---
title: "flat_teaching.Rmd for working package"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

<!-- 
Run this 'development' chunk

Store every call to library() that you need to run chunks line by line, as in a classical Rmd for analysis
-->

```{r development, include=FALSE}
library(testthat)
library(roxygen2)
```

<!--
# Description of your package

This will fill the description of your package.
Fill and run the content of this chunk, before anything else. 

Note: when you will use other flat templates, this part will be in a separate file. Do not be surprised!
--> 

```{r description, eval=FALSE}
# Describe your package
fusen::fill_description(
  pkg = here::here(),
  fields = list(
    Title = "Fresh and Efficient Data Processing Tools",
    Description = "`mintyr` is an R package offering fresh and efficient solutions for data processing, with a focus on animal breeding data. It provides streamlined functionality for handling multi-breed and multi-trait data in genomic selection, leveraging nested data structures for clear model iteration. Built primarily with `data.table` and base R for performance, the package delivers a comprehensive collection of utility functions that balance efficiency with user-friendly data manipulation approaches.Detailed documentation and examples are available through the package website at <https://tony2015116.github.io/mintyr>.",
    `Authors@R` = c(
      person("Guo Meng", email = "tony2015116@163.com", role = c("aut", "cre")),
      person(given = "Guo Meng", role = "cph")
    )
  ), overwrite = TRUE
)
# Define License with use_*_license()
usethis::use_mit_license("Guo Meng")
```



# split_cv
    
```{r function-split_cv}
#' Apply Cross-Validation to a List of Datasets
#'
#' @description
#' `split_cv` applies `rsample::vfold_cv` to each dataset in a named or
#' unnamed list, returning a list of `data.table` objects that each contain
#' the CV split objects alongside the corresponding training and validation
#' sets.
#'
#' @param split_dt A `list` whose every element is a `data.frame` or
#'   `data.table`. Must be non-empty.
#' @param v        Number of folds. Must be a single integer >= 2.
#'   Default is `10`.
#' @param repeats  Number of repeats. Must be a single integer >= 1.
#'   Default is `1`.
#' @param strata   A single character string naming the stratification column.
#'   The column must exist in **every** dataset. Set to `NULL` for no
#'   stratification. Default is `NULL`.
#' @param breaks   Number of bins when stratifying a numeric variable. Used
#'   only when `strata` is non-`NULL`. Default is `4`.
#' @param pool     Proportion threshold for pooling small strata. Used only
#'   when `strata` is non-`NULL`. Default is `0.1`.
#' @param ...      Additional arguments forwarded to `rsample::vfold_cv`.
#'
#' @details
#' For each dataset in `split_dt` the function:
#' \enumerate{
#'   \item Validates inputs once before entering the processing loop.
#'   \item Builds a `vfold_cv` argument list, appending stratification
#'         parameters only when `strata` is non-`NULL` to avoid passing
#'         unsupported arguments to `rsample`.
#'   \item Converts the rsample tibble to a `data.table` in a single
#'         `as.data.table()` call, preserving all fold-identifier columns
#'         (`id`, `id2`) without hard-coding on the value of `repeats`.
#'   \item Appends `train` and `validate` list-columns by reference via `:=`.
#' }
#'
#' @return A `list` of `data.table` objects (one per input dataset), each
#'   containing:
#' \itemize{
#'   \item `splits`   — rsample split objects.
#'   \item `id`       — fold identifier (always present).
#'   \item `id2`      — repeat identifier (present only when `repeats > 1`).
#'   \item `train`    — list-column of training data frames.
#'   \item `validate` — list-column of validation data frames.
#' }
#' The output list preserves the names of `split_dt`.
#'
#' @note
#' \itemize{
#'   \item When `strata` is specified, it must exist in **all** datasets;
#'         a missing column raises an error rather than silently falling back
#'         to unstratified CV.
#'   \item `breaks` and `pool` are forwarded to `rsample::vfold_cv` only
#'         when `strata` is non-`NULL`, preventing invalid-argument errors.
#'   \item `as.data.table()` on an already-`data.table` input is a no-op
#'         (no copy is made).
#' }
#'
#' @seealso
#' \itemize{
#'   \item [`rsample::vfold_cv()`]  — underlying cross-validation function
#'   \item [`rsample::training()`]  — extract training set from a split
#'   \item [`rsample::testing()`]   — extract validation set from a split
#'   \item [`nest_cv()`]            — nested data.table variant of this utility
#' }
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @importFrom rsample vfold_cv training testing
#' @export
split_cv <- function(split_dt,
                     v       = 10L,
                     repeats = 1L,
                     strata  = NULL,
                     breaks  = 4L,
                     pool    = 0.1,
                     ...) {

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  0. Suppress R CMD CHECK global binding notes
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  splits <- train <- validate <- NULL

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  1. Input validation (performed once, before the processing loop)
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  if (!is.list(split_dt)) {
    stop("`split_dt` must be a list. Received: ",
         paste(class(split_dt), collapse = "/"))
  }

  if (length(split_dt) == 0L) {
    stop("`split_dt` must not be empty.")
  }

  if (!is.numeric(v) || length(v) != 1L || v < 2L) {
    stop("`v` must be a single integer >= 2.")
  }

  if (!is.numeric(repeats) || length(repeats) != 1L || repeats < 1L) {
    stop("`repeats` must be a single integer >= 1.")
  }

  # Verify every element inherits from data.frame (covers data.table as well)
  # vapply is preferred over sapply: return type is guaranteed to be logical(1)
  is_valid <- vapply(split_dt, inherits, logical(1L), what = "data.frame")
  if (!all(is_valid)) {
    bad_idx <- which(!is_valid)
    stop("All elements of `split_dt` must be data.frames or data.tables.\n",
         "  Invalid element(s) at index: ", paste(bad_idx, collapse = ", "),
         "\n  Types received: ",
         paste(vapply(split_dt[bad_idx],
                      function(x) class(x)[1L], character(1L)),
               collapse = ", "))
  }

  # Validate strata argument structure and column presence across all datasets
  if (!is.null(strata)) {
    if (!is.character(strata) || length(strata) != 1L) {
      stop("`strata` must be a single character string (column name).")
    }

    # Check column existence in every dataset before entering the loop;
    # failing early avoids partial processing of a multi-dataset list
    strata_missing <- vapply(split_dt, function(df) {
      !strata %in% names(df)
    }, logical(1L))

    if (any(strata_missing)) {
      missing_idx <- which(strata_missing)
      stop("The `strata` column '", strata,
           "' is absent from dataset(s) at index: ",
           paste(missing_idx, collapse = ", "),
           if (!is.null(names(split_dt))) {
             paste0(" (name(s): '",
                    paste(names(split_dt)[missing_idx], collapse = "', '"),
                    "')")
           })
    }
  }

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  2. Build the vfold_cv argument list once, outside the loop
  #     Strata-specific parameters are appended only when strata is non-NULL,
  #     preventing "unused argument" errors from rsample when strata = NULL
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  cv_args <- c(
    list(v = v, repeats = repeats),
    if (!is.null(strata)) list(strata = strata, breaks = breaks, pool = pool),
    list(...)
  )

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  3. Pre-allocate result list, preserving names from split_dt
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  result        <- vector("list", length(split_dt))
  names(result) <- names(split_dt)

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  4. Process each dataset
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  for (i in seq_along(split_dt)) {

    # as.data.table() is a no-op when input is already a data.table;
    # avoids the redundant is.data.table() + conditional branch
    current_data <- data.table::as.data.table(split_dt[[i]])

    # Generate CV splits for this dataset
    cv_obj <- do.call(rsample::vfold_cv, c(list(data = current_data), cv_args))

    # Convert the rsample tibble to data.table in one pass.
    # This naturally retains whichever id columns rsample produced
    # (id only for repeats = 1; both id and id2 for repeats > 1),
    # eliminating the fragile hard-coded `if (repeats == 1)` branch.
    result_dt <- data.table::as.data.table(cv_obj)

    # Append train and validate list-columns by reference (no copy triggered).
    # Pass function references directly instead of wrapping in anonymous lambdas.
    result_dt[, `:=`(
      train    = lapply(splits, rsample::training),
      validate = lapply(splits, rsample::testing)
    )]

    result[[i]] <- result_dt
  }

  result
}
```
  
```{r example-split_cv}
# Prepare example data: Convert first 3 columns of iris dataset to long format and split
dt_split <- w2l_split(data = iris, cols2l = 1:3)
# dt_split is now a list containing 3 data tables for Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, and Petal.Length

# Example 1: Single cross-validation (no repeats)
split_cv(
  split_dt = dt_split,  # Input list of split data
  v = 3,                # Set 3-fold cross-validation
  repeats = 1           # Perform cross-validation once (no repeats)
)
# Returns a list where each element contains:
# - splits: rsample split objects
# - id: fold numbers (Fold1, Fold2, Fold3)
# - train: training set data
# - validate: validation set data

# Example 2: Repeated cross-validation
split_cv(
  split_dt = dt_split,  # Input list of split data
  v = 3,                # Set 3-fold cross-validation
  repeats = 2           # Perform cross-validation twice
)
# Returns a list where each element contains:
# - splits: rsample split objects
# - id: repeat numbers (Repeat1, Repeat2)
# - id2: fold numbers (Fold1, Fold2, Fold3)
# - train: training set data
# - validate: validation set data
```

# c2p_nest
    
```{r function-c2p_nest}
#' Column to Pair Nested Transformation
#'
#' @description
#' Generates combinations of specified columns and creates a nested data
#' structure based on these pairs. Each nested subset renames the combined
#' columns to \code{value1}, \code{value2}, ... (up to \code{pairs_n}) to
#' support uniform iterative analyses such as genetic correlation estimation.
#'
#' @param data A data.frame or data.table to be transformed.
#' @param cols2bind A character vector of column names or a numeric vector of
#'   column indices to be combined into pairs. Must not overlap with \code{by}.
#' @param by A character vector of column names or a numeric vector of column
#'   indices to group by. Default is \code{NULL}.
#' @param pairs_n A positive integer >= 2 indicating the size of each column
#'   combination (e.g., 2 for pairwise). Default is \code{2}.
#' @param sep A single character string used as a separator when constructing
#'   the \code{pairs} identifier column. Default is \code{"-"}.
#' @param nest_type A character string specifying the class of each nested
#'   object: \code{"dt"} (data.table, default) or \code{"df"} (data.frame).
#'
#' @details
#' The columns specified in \code{cols2bind} are renamed to \code{value1},
#' \code{value2}, ... within each nested subset. The original column names are
#' preserved in the \code{pairs} column (e.g., \code{"Sepal.Length-Sepal.Width"}),
#' ensuring full traceability for downstream iterative analyses such as genetic
#' correlation estimation.
#'
#' Columns that belong to neither \code{cols2bind} nor \code{by} (referred to
#' internally as "extra columns") are retained inside the nested subsets so
#' that covariates or ID fields remain accessible. Grouping columns (\code{by})
#' are \emph{not} duplicated inside the nested data because they are already
#' present as outer key columns in the returned table.
#'
#' When the number of requested combinations exceeds 500 a message is emitted;
#' above 5000 a warning is raised, as memory usage grows linearly with the
#' combination count.
#'
#' @return A \code{data.table} with columns:
#' \describe{
#'   \item{pairs}{Character. The column-combination identifier, e.g.
#'     \code{"Sepal.Length-Sepal.Width"}.}
#'   \item{...}{Any \code{by} grouping columns, one per variable.}
#'   \item{data}{List-column. Each cell holds a data.table (or data.frame when
#'     \code{nest_type = "df"}) containing \code{value1}, \code{value2}, ...,
#'     plus any extra columns that were neither in \code{cols2bind} nor
#'     \code{by}.}
#' }
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link[utils]{combn}} for the underlying combination generator.
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @importFrom utils combn
#' @export
c2p_nest <- function(data,
                     cols2bind,
                     by        = NULL,
                     pairs_n   = 2L,
                     sep       = "-",
                     nest_type = "dt") {
  . <- pairs <- NULL

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 1. Validate `data`
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  if (!inherits(data, c("data.table", "data.frame")))
    stop("'data' must be a data.table or data.frame.")

  dt <- data.table::as.data.table(data)   # always a fresh copy; safe to modify

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 2. Resolve and validate `cols2bind`
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  if (is.numeric(cols2bind)) {
    if (any(cols2bind < 1L | cols2bind > ncol(dt)))
      stop("'cols2bind' contains out-of-range column indices.")
    cols2bind <- names(dt)[as.integer(cols2bind)]
  }
  if (!is.character(cols2bind) || length(cols2bind) == 0L)
    stop("'cols2bind' must be a non-empty character or numeric vector.")

  missing_cols <- cols2bind[!cols2bind %in% names(dt)]
  if (length(missing_cols) > 0L)
    stop("Column(s) not found in 'data': ", paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", "))

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 3. Resolve and validate `by`
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  if (!is.null(by)) {
    if (is.numeric(by)) {
      if (any(by < 1L | by > ncol(dt)))
        stop("'by' contains out-of-range column indices.")
      by <- names(dt)[as.integer(by)]
    }
    if (!is.character(by) || length(by) == 0L)
      stop("'by' must be a non-empty character or numeric vector, or NULL.")

    missing_by <- by[!by %in% names(dt)]
    if (length(missing_by) > 0L)
      stop("Grouping column(s) not found in 'data': ", paste(missing_by, collapse = ", "))

    # FIX: detect overlap between cols2bind and by - previously silent,
    # now an explicit error to prevent malformed fixed_cols computation.
    overlap <- intersect(cols2bind, by)
    if (length(overlap) > 0L)
      stop("Column(s) appear in both 'cols2bind' and 'by': ",
           paste(overlap, collapse = ", "))
  }

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 4. Validate scalar arguments
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  pairs_n <- suppressWarnings(as.integer(pairs_n))
  if (is.na(pairs_n) || pairs_n < 2L)
    stop("'pairs_n' must be an integer >= 2.")
  if (pairs_n > length(cols2bind))
    stop(sprintf("'pairs_n' (%d) cannot exceed the number of 'cols2bind' columns (%d).",
                 pairs_n, length(cols2bind)))

  if (!is.character(sep) || length(sep) != 1L)
    stop("'sep' must be a single character string.")

  if (!nest_type %in% c("dt", "df"))
    stop("'nest_type' must be either \"dt\" or \"df\".")

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 5. Combination count guard
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  n_combs <- choose(length(cols2bind), pairs_n)
  if (n_combs > 5000L)
    warning(sprintf(
      "%d combinations requested. Memory usage will be high - consider reducing 'cols2bind' or 'pairs_n'.",
      n_combs))
  else if (n_combs > 500L)
    message(sprintf(
      "Note: generating %d combinations - this may take a moment.", n_combs))

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 6. Determine which columns travel inside the nested subsets
  #
  #    FIX: original code used setdiff(names(dt), cols2bind) which kept
  #    `by` columns inside every nested data object, duplicating them
  #    redundantly (they already appear as outer key columns).  The
  #    corrected definition keeps only truly "extra" columns - those that
  #    are neither combination targets nor grouping keys.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  extra_cols <- setdiff(names(dt), c(cols2bind, by))

  # value-name template reused across iterations
  value_names <- paste0("value", seq_len(pairs_n))

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 7. Build one nested data.table per combination
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  comb_list <- utils::combn(cols2bind, pairs_n, simplify = FALSE)

  nested_list <- lapply(comb_list, function(comb) {

    keep <- c(by, extra_cols, comb)          # column selection for this iter

    # Independent copy per iteration so setnames does not mutate `dt`
    dt_sub <- data.table::copy(dt[, keep, with = FALSE])

    # Rename combination columns to value1, value2, ... (intentional design:
    # uniform names support iterative genetic-correlation pipelines; the
    # `pairs` column retains the original name mapping for traceability).
    data.table::setnames(dt_sub, comb, value_names)

    # Nest: group by `by` (NULL -> whole table as one group)
    if (nest_type == "dt") {
      res <- dt_sub[, .(data = list(.SD)), by = by]
    } else {
      res <- dt_sub[, .(data = list(as.data.frame(.SD))), by = by]
    }

    res[, pairs := paste(comb, collapse = sep)]
    res
  })

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 8. Combine and reorder columns
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  result <- data.table::rbindlist(nested_list, use.names = TRUE, fill = TRUE)

  # Put `pairs` first, then `by` cols, then `data` - constructed explicitly
  # so no post-hoc setcolorder reshuffling is needed.
  target_order <- c("pairs", by, "data")
  data.table::setcolorder(result, target_order)

  result
}
```


```{r example-c2p_nest}
# Example data preparation: Define column names for combination
col_names <- c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length")

# Example 1: Basic column-to-pairs nesting with custom separator
c2p_nest(
  iris,                   # Input iris dataset
  cols2bind = col_names,  # Columns to be combined as pairs
  pairs_n = 2,            # Create pairs of 2 columns
  sep = "&"               # Custom separator for pair names
)
# Returns a nested data.table where:
# - pairs: combined column names (e.g., "Sepal.Length&Sepal.Width")
# - data: list column containing data.tables with value1, value2 columns

# Example 2: Column-to-pairs nesting with numeric indices and grouping
c2p_nest(
  iris,                   # Input iris dataset
  cols2bind = 1:3,        # First 3 columns to be combined
  pairs_n = 2,            # Create pairs of 2 columns
  by = 5                  # Group by 5th column (Species)
)
# Returns a nested data.table where:
# - pairs: combined column names
# - Species: grouping variable
# - data: list column containing data.tables grouped by Species
```

```{r example-c2p_nest}
# Example data preparation: Define column names for combination
col_names <- c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length")

# Example 1: Basic column-to-pairs nesting with custom separator
c2p_nest(
  iris,                   # Input iris dataset
  cols2bind = col_names,  # Columns to be combined as pairs
  pairs_n = 2,            # Create pairs of 2 columns
  sep = "&"               # Custom separator for pair names
)
# Returns a nested data.table where:
# - pairs: combined column names (e.g., "Sepal.Length&Sepal.Width")
# - data: list column containing data.tables with value1, value2 columns

# Example 2: Column-to-pairs nesting with numeric indices and grouping
c2p_nest(
  iris,                   # Input iris dataset
  cols2bind = 1:3,        # First 3 columns to be combined
  pairs_n = 2,            # Create pairs of 2 columns
  by = 5                  # Group by 5th column (Species)
)
# Returns a nested data.table where:
# - pairs: combined column names
# - Species: grouping variable
# - data: list column containing data.tables grouped by Species
```

# r2p_nest
    
```{r function-r2p_nest}
#' Row to Pair Nested Transformation
#'
#' @description
#' A sophisticated data transformation tool for performing row pair conversion
#' and creating nested data structures. It smartly iterates through variables
#' to perfectly preserve non-target contextual variables while utilizing
#' native `dcast` for extreme performance.
#'
#' @param data Input `data frame` or `data table`.
#' @param rows2bind A character column name or numeric index to be used as row values.
#' @param by A character vector or numeric vector of column indices to transform.
#' @param nest_type Output nesting format (`"dt"` or `"df"`). Default `"dt"`.
#'
#' @return A nested `data.table` containing `name` and `data` columns, with
#'   all contextual features preserved inside the nested structures.
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @importFrom stats as.formula
#' @export
#' @examples
#' # Example: Row-to-pairs nesting with column names
#' r2p_nest(
#'   mtcars,
#'   rows2bind = "cyl",
#'   by = c("hp", "drat", "wt")
#' )
r2p_nest <- function(data, rows2bind, by, nest_type = "dt") {
  . <- name <- NULL

  # 1. 基础校验与转换
  if (length(by) < 1) stop("At least one column must be specified in 'by'")
  if (length(rows2bind) != 1) stop("rows2bind must be a single column")

  if (!inherits(data, c("data.frame", "data.table"))) {
    stop("data must be a data.table or data.frame")
  }

  # 转换为 data.table 进行极速操作
  dt <- if (data.table::is.data.table(data)) data.table::copy(data) else data.table::as.data.table(data)

  # 2. 列名映射与存在性校验
  if (is.numeric(by)) {
    if (any(by > ncol(dt) | by < 1)) stop("Invalid column indices in by")
    by <- names(dt)[by]
  } else if (!all(by %in% names(dt))) {
    stop("Specified by columns not found in data")
  }

  if (is.numeric(rows2bind)) {
    if (rows2bind > ncol(dt) | rows2bind < 1) stop("Invalid column index in rows2bind")
    rows2bind <- names(dt)[rows2bind]
  } else if (!rows2bind %in% names(dt)) {
    stop("Specified rows2bind not found in data")
  }

  if (!nest_type %in% c("dt", "df")) stop("Invalid nest_type. Must be 'dt' or 'df'.")

  # 3. 核心重构：保留循环实现隔离，完美保留上下文列
  result_list <- lapply(by, function(b) {

    # 核心逻辑：获取除了当前处理变量 b 和目标 pivot 变量 rows2bind 以外的所有列。
    # 这确保了当处理 'hp' 时，'drat' 和 'wt' 以及其他无关列都会作为 ID 列被稳稳保留。
    other_ids <- setdiff(names(dt), c(b, rows2bind))

    # 动态构建 dcast 极速透视公式
    if (length(other_ids) == 0) {
      form_str <- sprintf(". ~ `%s`", rows2bind)
    } else {
      # 增加反引号包裹，防止遇到带空格或特殊符号的列名时报错
      safe_other_ids <- sprintf("`%s`", other_ids)
      form_str <- sprintf("%s ~ `%s`", paste(safe_other_ids, collapse = " + "), rows2bind)
    }

    # 极速 Dcast：将原本拖沓的 melt 步骤省略，直接指定 value.var
    wide_dt <- data.table::dcast(dt, stats::as.formula(form_str), value.var = b)

    # 标注当前的分组变量来源
    wide_dt[, name := b]

    # 按照嵌套格式进行原生分组压缩
    if (nest_type == "dt") {
      res <- wide_dt[, .(data = list(.SD)), by = "name"]
    } else {
      res <- wide_dt[, .(data = list(as.data.frame(.SD))), by = "name"]
    }

    return(res)
  })

  # 4. 汇总返回
  combined_result <- data.table::rbindlist(result_list)
  return(combined_result)
}
```

```{r example-r2p_nest}
# Example 1: Row-to-pairs nesting with column names
r2p_nest(
  mtcars,                     # Input mtcars dataset
  rows2bind = "cyl",          # Column to be used as row values
  by = c("hp", "drat", "wt")  # Columns to be transformed into pairs
)
# Returns a nested data.table where:
# - name: variable names (hp, drat, wt)
# - data: list column containing data.tables with rows grouped by cyl values

# Example 2: Row-to-pairs nesting with numeric indices
r2p_nest(
  mtcars,                     # Input mtcars dataset
  rows2bind = 2,              # Use 2nd column (cyl) as row values
  by = 4:6                    # Use columns 4-6 (hp, drat, wt) for pairs
)
# Returns a nested data.table where:
# - name: variable names from columns 4-6
# - data: list column containing data.tables with rows grouped by cyl values
```

# export_nest
    
```{r function-export_nest}
#' Export Nested Data Structures with Hierarchical Directory Organization
#'
#' @description
#' Exports list-columns containing \code{data.frame} or \code{data.table} objects from a
#' \code{data.frame}/\code{data.table} to \code{txt} or \code{csv} files, automatically
#' constructing a hierarchical directory structure from non-nested columns.
#' Exportable nested columns (those holding \code{data.frame}/\code{data.table} elements)
#' are distinguished from non-exportable custom-object columns (e.g. \code{rsplit} from
#' the rsample package); only the former are written to disk by default.
#'
#' @param nest_dt     A \code{data.frame} or \code{data.table} containing at least one
#'   nested list-column. Must have one or more rows.
#' @param group_cols  Optional character vector of column names used to build the
#'   hierarchical output directory structure. When \code{NULL} (default), all non-nested
#'   columns are used automatically.
#' @param nest_cols   Optional character vector of nested column names to export. When
#'   \code{NULL} (default), all columns whose elements are \code{data.frame}/\code{data.table}
#'   objects are exported automatically; custom-object list-columns are reported and skipped.
#'   Specifying a non-\code{data.frame} column triggers a warning and that column is skipped.
#' @param export_path Single character string specifying the root export directory.
#'   Defaults to \code{tempdir()}. Created recursively if it does not exist.
#' @param file_type   Either \code{"txt"} (tab-separated, default) or \code{"csv"}
#'   (comma-separated). Case-insensitive.
#'
#' @return An invisible \code{integer} giving the total number of files successfully written.
#'   Returns \code{0L} when no exportable columns are found or all nested data are empty/\code{NULL}.
#'
#' @details
#' **Nested column classification (mutually exclusive):**
#' \itemize{
#'   \item \emph{Exportable} — every element inherits from \code{data.frame} or \code{data.table}.
#'   \item \emph{Non-exportable} — empty lists or elements of any other class
#'     (e.g. \code{rsplit}, \code{vfold_split}). Reported to the console; never written.
#' }
#'
#' **Directory layout:**
#' \code{export_path / <group1_value> / <group2_value> / <nest_col_name>.<file_type>}
#'
#' **Performance notes:**
#' \itemize{
#'   \item Row data is accessed via \code{.subset2()} (zero-copy column access) rather than
#'     \code{nest_dt[i]}, eliminating per-row \code{data.table} allocation in the hot loop.
#'   \item All \code{n} output directory paths are pre-computed in a single vectorised
#'     \code{do.call(file.path, ...)} call before the loop; only the \code{k} unique paths
#'     are then passed to \code{dir.create()}, replacing \code{n} syscalls with \code{k}
#'     (\code{k <= n}; often \code{k << n} when many rows share the same group).
#'   \item The field separator and output filenames are computed once before the loop.
#'   \item \code{seq_len()} is used instead of \code{1:n} to avoid the \code{1:0} edge-case bug.
#'   \item All list-column introspection uses \code{vapply} with explicit \code{FUN.VALUE} to
#'     guarantee return types and prevent silent coercion.
#' }
#'
#' @section Dependencies:
#' Requires the \pkg{data.table} package for data manipulation and file I/O (\code{fwrite}).
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link[data.table]{fwrite}}
#'
#' @importFrom data.table is.data.table as.data.table fwrite
#' @export
export_nest <- function(nest_dt,
                        group_cols  = NULL,
                        nest_cols   = NULL,
                        export_path = tempdir(),
                        file_type   = "txt") {

  # ── 0. Dependency check ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!requireNamespace("data.table", quietly = TRUE)) {
    stop("Package 'data.table' is required. Install it with install.packages('data.table').")
  }

  # ── 1. Coerce input to data.table ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Convert once up-front; all subsequent operations assume data.table semantics.
  if (!data.table::is.data.table(nest_dt)) {
    nest_dt <- data.table::as.data.table(nest_dt)
  }

  # ── 2. Early parameter validation (fail-fast) ─────────────────────────────────
  if (nrow(nest_dt) == 0L) {
    stop("'nest_dt' must not be empty (nrow == 0).")
  }

  if (!is.character(export_path) || length(export_path) != 1L || is.na(export_path)) {
    stop("'export_path' must be a single non-NA character string.")
  }

  # Normalise file_type and resolve separator once — avoids recomputation in the hot loop.
  file_type <- tolower(trimws(file_type))
  if (!file_type %in% c("txt", "csv")) {
    stop("'file_type' must be either \"txt\" or \"csv\".")
  }
  sep <- if (file_type == "txt") "\t" else ","

  # ── 3. Nested column detection (mutually exclusive classification) ─────────────
  # Helper: returns TRUE only when every element of a list-column inherits from
  # data.frame or data.table.  Uses vapply (strict return type) to guard against
  # silent coercion and the all(logical(0)) == TRUE pitfall on empty lists.
  .is_df_list_col <- function(x) {
    if (!is.list(x) || length(x) == 0L) return(FALSE)
    all(vapply(x,
               function(elem) inherits(elem, c("data.frame", "data.table")),
               FUN.VALUE = logical(1L)))
  }

  # Step 1: Identify ALL list-columns (empty lists, custom objects, df/dt columns).
  all_list_cols <- names(nest_dt)[vapply(nest_dt, is.list, FUN.VALUE = logical(1L))]

  if (length(all_list_cols) == 0L) {
    stop("'nest_dt' contains no list-columns (nested columns).")
  }

  # Step 2: Among all list-columns, find those holding data.frame/data.table elements.
  df_nested_cols <- all_list_cols[vapply(nest_dt[, all_list_cols, with = FALSE],
                                         .is_df_list_col,
                                         FUN.VALUE = logical(1L))]

  # Step 3: Remaining list-columns are strictly non-exportable (setdiff guarantees
  # mutual exclusivity — no overlap with df_nested_cols).
  other_list_cols <- setdiff(all_list_cols, df_nested_cols)

  # ── 4. Resolve which nested columns to export ─────────────────────────────────
  if (is.null(nest_cols)) {
    # Auto mode: export only data.frame/data.table nested columns.
    if (length(df_nested_cols) == 0L) {
      stop("No data.frame/data.table nested columns found. ",
           "Specify 'nest_cols' explicitly or convert list-column elements first.")
    }
    nest_cols <- df_nested_cols
    message("[ export_nest ] Auto-selected data.frame/data.table nested columns: ",
            paste(nest_cols, collapse = ", "))
    if (length(other_list_cols) > 0L) {
      message("[ export_nest ] The following columns contain custom objects and will be skipped ",
              "(cannot be written as txt/csv): ",
              paste(other_list_cols, collapse = ", "))
    }
  } else {
    # Manual mode: validate user-supplied column names.
    if (!is.character(nest_cols) || length(nest_cols) == 0L) {
      stop("'nest_cols' must be a non-empty character vector.")
    }
    # Ensure all specified columns actually exist.
    invalid_cols <- setdiff(nest_cols, names(nest_dt))
    if (length(invalid_cols) > 0L) {
      stop("The following columns specified in 'nest_cols' do not exist in 'nest_dt': ",
           paste(invalid_cols, collapse = ", "))
    }
    # Drop any columns that do not hold data.frame/data.table elements.
    non_df_cols <- setdiff(nest_cols, df_nested_cols)
    if (length(non_df_cols) > 0L) {
      warning("The following columns do not contain data.frame/data.table objects ",
              "and will be skipped: ",
              paste(non_df_cols, collapse = ", "), call. = FALSE)
      nest_cols <- intersect(nest_cols, df_nested_cols)
    }
    if (length(nest_cols) == 0L) {
      stop("No valid data.frame/data.table columns remain after filtering.")
    }
  }

  # ── 5. Resolve grouping columns ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (is.null(group_cols)) {
    # Auto mode: every non-list column becomes a grouping variable.
    group_cols <- setdiff(names(nest_dt), all_list_cols)
    if (length(group_cols) > 0L) {
      message("[ export_nest ] Auto-selected grouping columns: ",
              paste(group_cols, collapse = ", "))
    } else {
      message("[ export_nest ] No grouping columns found; all files will be written to the root export path.")
    }
  } else {
    if (!is.character(group_cols) || length(group_cols) == 0L) {
      stop("'group_cols' must be a non-empty character vector (or NULL).")
    }
    missing_cols <- setdiff(group_cols, names(nest_dt))
    if (length(missing_cols) > 0L) {
      warning("The following 'group_cols' columns are absent and will be ignored: ",
              paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", "), call. = FALSE)
      group_cols <- intersect(group_cols, names(nest_dt))
    }
    if (length(group_cols) == 0L) {
      message("[ export_nest ] No valid grouping columns remain; files will be written to the root export path.")
    } else {
      message("[ export_nest ] Using grouping columns: ", paste(group_cols, collapse = ", "))
    }
  }

  # ── 6. Pre-compute output filenames (once, outside the hot loop) ──────────────
  # Pattern: {nested_column_name}.{file_type}, e.g. "data.txt", "results.csv"
  filenames        <- paste0(nest_cols, ".", file_type)
  names(filenames) <- nest_cols   # Named vector enables O(1) lookup by column name.

  # ── 7. Vectorised path pre-computation + batch directory creation ─────────────
  # (Improvement merged from Gemini's version)
  #
  # Instead of building each row's directory path inside the hot loop and calling
  # dir.create() n times, we:
  #   (a) leverage file.path()'s vectorised behaviour via do.call() to generate
  #       ALL n directory paths in a single R call — no per-row overhead.
  #   (b) deduplicate with unique() and create only the distinct directories once,
  #       before the loop starts.  When n >> number of unique groups (a common case),
  #       this replaces n syscalls with k syscalls (k = unique group combinations).
  if (length(group_cols) > 0L) {
    # as.character() is applied column-wise to the group sub-table; lapply returns a
    # list of character vectors that do.call(file.path, ...) consumes as positional
    # arguments — fully vectorised, zero row-slice copies.
    group_paths <- do.call(
      file.path,
      c(list(export_path),
        lapply(nest_dt[, group_cols, with = FALSE], as.character))
    )
  } else {
    group_paths <- rep(export_path, nrow(nest_dt))
  }

  # Batch-create every unique subdirectory before entering the write loop.
  # dir.create() is a syscall; eliminating redundant calls matters at scale.
  lapply(unique(group_paths), dir.create, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

  # ── 8. Main export loop ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Design rationale:
  #   - seq_len(n) instead of 1:n — avoids the classic 1:0 == c(1, 0) edge case.
  #   - .subset2(nest_dt, col)[[i]] instead of nest_dt[i][[col]] — the latter copies
  #     the entire row into a new data.table on every iteration; .subset2 accesses
  #     the column vector directly (zero additional allocation).
  #   - dir_path is now looked up from the pre-computed group_paths vector (O(1))
  #     rather than being rebuilt per row.
  n_rows      <- nrow(nest_dt)
  total_count <- 0L

  for (i in seq_len(n_rows)) {

    # Retrieve the pre-computed directory path for this row — no string work at all.
    dir_path <- group_paths[i]

    # ---- Write each nested column for this row ----
    for (j in seq_along(nest_cols)) {
      col_name <- nest_cols[j]

      tryCatch({
        # Zero-copy element access: retrieve the list element directly from the
        # column vector without materialising a row-slice data.table.
        nested_elem <- .subset2(nest_dt, col_name)[[i]]

        # Skip NULL values and zero-row data frames silently.
        if (is.null(nested_elem)) next
        if (is.data.frame(nested_elem) && nrow(nested_elem) == 0L) next

        # Coerce to data.table. If already a data.table, as.data.table() passes by
        # reference (no copy); otherwise a single conversion occurs here.
        nested_dt <- data.table::as.data.table(nested_elem)

        # Write to disk using fwrite — the fastest tabular I/O in the data.table ecosystem.
        file_path <- file.path(dir_path, filenames[col_name])
        data.table::fwrite(nested_dt, file = file_path, sep = sep)

        total_count <- total_count + 1L

      }, error = function(e) {
        warning(sprintf("[ export_nest ] Failed to export column '%s' at row %d: %s",
                        col_name, i, conditionMessage(e)),
                call. = FALSE)
      })
    }
  }

  # ── 9. Summary message ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  message(sprintf("[ export_nest ] Export complete. %d file(s) written to: %s",
                  total_count, export_path))

  invisible(total_count)
}
```

```{r example-export_nest}
# Example 1: Basic nested data export workflow
# Step 1: Create nested data structure
dt_nest <- w2l_nest(
  data = iris,              # Input iris dataset
  cols2l = 1:2,             # Columns to be nested
  by = "Species"            # Grouping variable
)

# Step 2: Export nested data to files
export_nest(
  nest_dt = dt_nest,        # Input nested data.table
  nest_cols = "data",       # Column containing nested data
  group_cols = c("name", "Species")  # Columns to create directory structure
)
# Returns the number of files created
# Creates directory structure: tempdir()/name/Species/data.txt

# Check exported files
list.files(
  path = tempdir(),         # Default export directory
  pattern = "txt",          # File type pattern to search
  recursive = TRUE          # Search in subdirectories
)
# Returns list of created files and their paths

# Clean up exported files
files <- list.files(
  path = tempdir(),         # Default export directory
  pattern = "txt",          # File type pattern to search
  recursive = TRUE,         # Search in subdirectories
  full.names = TRUE         # Return full file paths
)
file.remove(files)          # Remove all exported files
```


# export_list
    
```{r function-export_list}
#' Export a List of Data Frames with Hierarchical Directory Management
#'
#' @description
#' Exports every element of a named (or unnamed) \code{list} of \code{data.frame} /
#' \code{data.table} objects to \code{txt} or \code{csv} files.  Element names may
#' contain forward-slashes (\code{/}) to encode arbitrary subdirectory depth, e.g.
#' \code{"group_a/subject_01/results"} writes
#' \code{<export_path>/group_a/subject_01/results.txt}.
#' Unnamed elements are automatically labelled \code{split_<i>}.
#'
#' @param split_dt    A non-empty \code{list} whose elements are \code{data.frame},
#'   \code{data.table}, or any object coercible via \code{data.table::as.data.table()}.
#' @param export_path Single character string - the root export directory.
#'   Created recursively if absent. Defaults to \code{tempdir()}.
#' @param file_type   \code{"txt"} (tab-separated, default) or \code{"csv"}
#'   (comma-separated). Case-insensitive.
#'
#' @return An invisible named \code{character} vector of the absolute file paths
#'   written, with length equal to the number of successfully exported elements.
#'   The total count is accessible via \code{length()} on the return value.
#'
#' @details
#' **Performance design:**
#' \itemize{
#'   \item All element names are resolved and path components split in a single
#'     vectorised pass \emph{before} the write loop, so no string work occurs
#'     inside the hot path.
#'   \item Unique subdirectories are collected and created in one batch
#'     (\code{k} \code{dir.create()} syscalls, where \code{k} \eqn{\le} \code{n}).
#'   \item The field separator is resolved once at function entry.
#'   \item \code{as.data.table()} on an existing \code{data.table} is a
#'     reference-pass (no copy).
#' }
#'
#' **Error handling:**
#' Individual element failures emit a \code{warning} and are skipped; the
#' remaining elements continue to be processed.
#'
#' @section Dependencies:
#' Requires the \pkg{data.table} package.
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link[data.table]{fwrite}}
#'
#' @importFrom data.table is.data.table as.data.table fwrite
#' @export
export_list <- function(split_dt,
                        export_path = tempdir(),
                        file_type   = "txt") {

  # -- 0. Dependency check -------------------------------------------------------
  if (!requireNamespace("data.table", quietly = TRUE)) {
    stop("Package 'data.table' is required. Install with install.packages('data.table').")
  }

  # -- 1. Input validation (fail-fast) ------------------------------------------
  if (!is.list(split_dt)) {
    stop("'split_dt' must be a list of data.frame / data.table objects.")
  }
  if (length(split_dt) == 0L) {
    message("[ export_list ] 'split_dt' is empty - nothing to export.")
    return(invisible(character(0L)))
  }
  if (!is.character(export_path) || length(export_path) != 1L || is.na(export_path)) {
    stop("'export_path' must be a single non-NA character string.")
  }

  # Resolve separator once - never recompute inside the loop.
  file_type <- tolower(trimws(file_type))
  if (!file_type %in% c("txt", "csv")) {
    stop("'file_type' must be \"txt\" or \"csv\".")
  }
  sep <- if (file_type == "txt") "\t" else ","

  # -- 2. Vectorised name resolution ---------------------------------------------
  # Build a canonical character vector of element names in one pass.
  # Unnamed / empty-string elements fall back to "split_<i>".
  raw_names <- names(split_dt)
  elem_names <- vapply(
    seq_along(split_dt),
    function(i) {
      nm <- if (is.null(raw_names)) "" else raw_names[i]
      if (is.na(nm) || nchar(nm) == 0L) paste0("split_", i) else nm
    },
    FUN.VALUE = character(1L)
  )

  # -- 3. Vectorised path decomposition -----------------------------------------
  # Split every name on "/" once, outside the write loop.
  # Result: list of character vectors, one per element.
  path_parts <- strsplit(elem_names, "/", fixed = TRUE)

  # Derive the subdirectory and bare filename for each element.
  # file_names : character(n) - the last path component (no extension)
  # dir_paths  : character(n) - full absolute directory path
  n <- length(split_dt)

  file_names <- vapply(path_parts, function(p) p[length(p)],  FUN.VALUE = character(1L))
  sub_dirs   <- vapply(path_parts,
                       function(p) {
                         if (length(p) > 1L) paste(p[-length(p)], collapse = "/") else ""
                       },
                       FUN.VALUE = character(1L))

  dir_paths <- ifelse(
    nchar(sub_dirs) > 0L,
    file.path(export_path, sub_dirs),
    export_path
  )

  # -- 4. Batch directory creation -----------------------------------------------
  # Always ensure the root exists, then create every unique subdirectory in one
  # batch - k syscalls instead of n (k = number of unique directories, k <= n).
  all_dirs <- unique(c(export_path, dir_paths))
  lapply(all_dirs, dir.create, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

  # -- 5. Pre-compute full file paths (once, before the write loop) --------------
  full_paths <- file.path(dir_paths, paste0(file_names, ".", file_type))

  # -- 6. Write loop -------------------------------------------------------------
  # - seq_len() avoids the 1:0 == c(1,0) edge case.
  # - Individual failures are caught and warned; processing continues.
  # - as.data.table() on an existing data.table is a zero-copy reference pass.
  # - Accumulate successfully written paths for the return value.
  written_paths <- character(n)   # Pre-allocate; trim after the loop.
  write_count   <- 0L

  for (i in seq_len(n)) {
    tryCatch({
      dt_elem <- data.table::as.data.table(split_dt[[i]])

      # Skip zero-row elements silently - nothing meaningful to write.
      if (nrow(dt_elem) == 0L) next

      data.table::fwrite(dt_elem, file = full_paths[i], sep = sep, quote = TRUE)

      write_count <- write_count + 1L
      written_paths[write_count] <- full_paths[i]

    }, error = function(e) {
      warning(sprintf("[ export_list ] Failed to export element %d ('%s'): %s",
                      i, elem_names[i], conditionMessage(e)),
              call. = FALSE)
    })
  }

  # Trim pre-allocated vector to actual written count.
  written_paths <- written_paths[seq_len(write_count)]
  names(written_paths) <- elem_names[match(written_paths, full_paths)]

  # -- 7. Summary ----------------------------------------------------------------
  message(sprintf("[ export_list ] Export complete. %d / %d file(s) written to: %s",
                  write_count, n, export_path))

  invisible(written_paths)
}
```
  
```{r example-export_list}
# Example: Export split data to files

# Step 1: Create split data structure
dt_split <- w2l_split(
  data = iris,              # Input iris dataset
  cols2l = 1:2,             # Columns to be split
  by = "Species"            # Grouping variable
)

# Step 2: Export split data to files
export_list(
  split_dt = dt_split       # Input list of data.tables
)
# Returns the number of files created
# Files are saved in tempdir() with .txt extension

# Check exported files
list.files(
  path = tempdir(),         # Default export directory
  pattern = "txt",          # File type pattern to search
  recursive = TRUE          # Search in subdirectories
)

# Clean up exported files
files <- list.files(
  path = tempdir(),         # Default export directory
  pattern = "txt",          # File type pattern to search
  recursive = TRUE,         # Search in subdirectories
  full.names = TRUE         # Return full file paths
)
file.remove(files)          # Remove all exported files
```

# format_digits
    
```{r function-format_digits}
#' Format Numeric Columns to Fixed-Decimal Character Strings
#'
#' @param data        A data.frame or data.table. The input dataset.
#' @param cols        A character or integer vector specifying columns to format.
#'                    If NULL (default), all numeric columns are formatted.
#' @param digits      A non-negative integer specifying decimal places. Defaults to 2.
#' @param percentage  Logical. If TRUE, values are multiplied by 100 and a "%"
#'                    sign is appended. Defaults to FALSE.
#' @param nan_as_na   Logical. If TRUE, NaN is treated identically to NA and
#'                    coerced to NA_character_. If FALSE (default), NaN is
#'                    preserved as the string "NaN".
#'
#' @return A data.table with the specified numeric columns formatted as character
#'         strings. The original object is never modified.
#'
#' @details
#' The function processes columns in the following order:
#' \enumerate{
#'   \item Validates all input parameters with informative error messages.
#'   \item Copies the input only once: data.table inputs are deep-copied via
#'         \code{copy()}; data.frame inputs are copied implicitly by
#'         \code{as.data.table()}, avoiding a redundant second copy.
#'   \item Resolves \code{cols} to a character vector of valid numeric column
#'         names, warning and skipping any non-numeric columns specified.
#'   \item Applies a vectorised formatting function via
#'         \code{lapply(.SD, fn)} and \code{:=}, so all target columns are
#'         dispatched in a single data.table call rather than a column-by-column
#'         loop.
#' }
#'
#' NA and NaN handling:
#' \itemize{
#'   \item \code{NA_real_} is always returned as \code{NA_character_}.
#'   \item \code{NaN} is returned as \code{"NaN"} by default. Set
#'         \code{nan_as_na = TRUE} to coerce it to \code{NA_character_} instead.
#' }
#'
#' Rounding uses explicit \code{round()} before \code{sprintf()} to guarantee
#' consistent results across platforms (Windows, Linux, macOS), where the
#' underlying C library's rounding behaviour may otherwise differ.
#'
#' @note
#' \itemize{
#'   \item \code{data} must be a data.frame or data.table.
#'   \item Integer column indices in \code{cols} are converted to column names
#'         internally; duplicates are silently removed.
#'   \item \code{digits} accepts numeric values such as \code{2.0} and coerces
#'         them to integer; non-integer-valued numbers raise an error.
#'   \item The function depends only on \code{data.table} and base R.
#' }
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @export
format_digits <- function(data,
                          cols       = NULL,
                          digits     = 2L,
                          percentage = FALSE,
                          nan_as_na  = FALSE) {

  # -- 1. Input validation ---------------------------------------------------
  if (!is.data.frame(data)) {
    stop("'data' must be a data.frame or data.table.", call. = FALSE)
  }
  if (!is.logical(percentage) || length(percentage) != 1L || is.na(percentage)) {
    stop("'percentage' must be a single non-NA logical value (TRUE or FALSE).",
         call. = FALSE)
  }
  if (!is.logical(nan_as_na) || length(nan_as_na) != 1L || is.na(nan_as_na)) {
    stop("'nan_as_na' must be a single non-NA logical value (TRUE or FALSE).",
         call. = FALSE)
  }

  # -- 2. Coerce digits to integer (tolerates inputs like 2.0) --------------
  digits <- suppressWarnings(as.integer(digits))
  if (is.na(digits) || length(digits) != 1L || digits < 0L) {
    stop("'digits' must be a single non-negative integer (e.g. 0, 1, 2, ...).",
         call. = FALSE)
  }

  # -- 3. Single-copy strategy -----------------------------------------------
  # data.table input  -> copy() performs one explicit deep copy.
  # data.frame input  -> as.data.table() allocates a new object; no second
  #                      copy() needed, avoiding the double-allocation present
  #                      in the original implementation.
  result <- if (is.data.table(data)) copy(data) else as.data.table(data)

  # -- 4. Resolve cols to a valid character vector of column names -----------
  if (is.null(cols)) {
    # vapply with a fixed return type is safer and faster than sapply
    target_cols <- names(result)[vapply(result, is.numeric, logical(1L))]

  } else {
    if (!is.numeric(cols) && !is.character(cols)) {
      stop("'cols' must be a character vector of column names or an integer ",
           "vector of column indices.", call. = FALSE)
    }
    if (length(cols) == 0L) {
      stop("'cols' must not be empty. Pass NULL to format all numeric columns.",
           call. = FALSE)
    }

    # Convert integer indices to column names
    if (is.numeric(cols)) {
      cols    <- as.integer(cols)
      bad_idx <- cols < 1L | cols > ncol(result)
      if (any(bad_idx)) {
        stop("Column indices out of range (valid range: 1-", ncol(result), "): ",
             paste(cols[bad_idx], collapse = ", "), call. = FALSE)
      }
      cols <- names(result)[cols]
    }

    # Drop duplicates to prevent undefined behaviour from repeated := assignment
    cols <- unique(cols)

    # Verify all names exist
    missing_cols <- setdiff(cols, names(result))
    if (length(missing_cols) > 0L) {
      stop("The following columns were not found in 'data': ",
           paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", "), call. = FALSE)
    }

    # Use .subset2 to extract each column vector directly, bypassing the
    # [.data.table dispatch overhead - measurably faster on wide tables.
    is_num <- vapply(cols,
                     function(cn) is.numeric(.subset2(result, cn)),
                     logical(1L))
    non_numeric <- cols[!is_num]
    if (length(non_numeric) > 0L) {
      warning("The following columns are not numeric and will be skipped: ",
              paste(non_numeric, collapse = ", "), call. = FALSE)
      cols <- cols[is_num]
    }
    if (length(cols) == 0L) {
      message("No formattable numeric columns remain. Returning a copy of the input.")
      return(result)
    }
    target_cols <- cols
  }

  # -- 5. Edge case: no numeric columns present ------------------------------
  if (length(target_cols) == 0L) {
    message("'data' contains no numeric columns. Returning a copy of the input.")
    return(result)
  }

  # -- 6. Build the format template once; reuse across all target columns ----
  fmt_tpl <- paste0("%.", digits, "f", if (percentage) "%%" else "")

  # -- 7. Vectorised formatting function -------------------------------------
  # Design rationale:
  #   a) Explicit round() before sprintf() ensures identical rounding across
  #      platforms; sprintf alone delegates to the C library, whose tie-breaking
  #      behaviour differs between Windows, Linux, and macOS.
  #   b) NA_real_ and NaN are handled separately:
  #        - NA_real_ -> always NA_character_
  #        - NaN      -> "NaN" by default; NA_character_ when nan_as_na = TRUE
  #   c) data.table::fifelse (C-level) is used instead of base ifelse to avoid
  #      R-level type checking and recycling logic (~3-5x faster on large vectors).
  fmt_fn <- if (percentage) {
    function(x) {
      val <- sprintf(fmt_tpl, round(x * 100, digits))
      val <- fifelse(is.na(x) & !is.nan(x), NA_character_, val)
      if (nan_as_na) val <- fifelse(is.nan(x), NA_character_, val)
      val
    }
  } else {
    function(x) {
      val <- sprintf(fmt_tpl, round(x, digits))
      val <- fifelse(is.na(x) & !is.nan(x), NA_character_, val)
      if (nan_as_na) val <- fifelse(is.nan(x), NA_character_, val)
      val
    }
  }

  # -- 8. Batch in-place update via data.table's := -------------------------
  # lapply(.SD, fmt_fn) dispatches all target columns in a single data.table
  # call, reducing R->C layer transitions compared to a for + set() loop.
  result[, (target_cols) := lapply(.SD, fmt_fn), .SDcols = target_cols][]

  return(result)
}
```
  
```{r example-format_digits}
# Example: Number formatting demonstrations

# Setup test data
dt <- data.table::data.table(
  a = c(0.1234, 0.5678),      # Numeric column 1
  b = c(0.2345, 0.6789),      # Numeric column 2
  c = c("text1", "text2")     # Text column
)

# Example 1: Format all numeric columns
format_digits(
  dt,                         # Input data table
  digits = 2                  # Round to 2 decimal places
)

# Example 2: Format specific column as percentage
format_digits(
  dt,                         # Input data table
  cols = c("a"),              # Only format column 'a'
  digits = 2,                 # Round to 2 decimal places
  percentage = TRUE           # Convert to percentage
)
```


# mintyr_example
    
```{r function-mintyr_example}
#' Get path to mintyr examples
#' 
#' `mintyr` comes bundled with a number of sample files in
#' its `inst/extdata` directory. Use `mintyr_example()` to retrieve the full file path to a 
#' specific example file.
#' 
#' @param path Name of the example file to locate. If NULL or missing,
#'   returns the directory path containing the examples.
#' @return Character string containing the full path to the requested example file.
#' @seealso [mintyr::mintyr_examples()] to list all available example files
#' @export
mintyr_example <- function(path = NULL) {
  # Handle case when path is NULL or missing
  if (is.null(path)) {
    return(system.file("extdata", package = "mintyr", mustWork = TRUE))
  }
  
  
  # Get the file path
  file_path <- system.file("extdata", path, package = "mintyr", mustWork = TRUE)
  
  return(file_path)
}
```
  
```{r example-mintyr_example}
# Get path to an example file
mintyr_example("csv_test1.csv")
```

# mintyr_examples
    
```{r function-mintyr_examples}
#' List all available example files in mintyr package
#' 
#' `mintyr` comes bundled with a number of sample files in its `inst/extdata` 
#' directory. This function lists all available example files, optionally filtered
#' by a pattern.
#' 
#' @param pattern A regular expression to filter filenames. If `NULL` (default),
#'   all available files are returned.
#' @return A character vector containing the names of example files. If no files
#'   match the pattern or if the example directory is empty, returns a zero-length
#'   character vector.
#' @seealso [mintyr::mintyr_example()] to get the full path of a specific example file
#' @export
mintyr_examples <- function(pattern = NULL) {
  # Validate pattern if provided
  if (!is.null(pattern)) {
    if (!is.character(pattern)) {
      stop("'pattern' must be a character string or NULL", call. = FALSE)
    }
  }
  
  # Get the example directory path
  example_dir <- system.file("extdata", package = "mintyr")
  
  # Check if directory exists
  if (example_dir == "") {
    return(character(0))
  }
  
  # Get and return matching files
  list.files(example_dir, pattern = pattern)
}
```
  
```{r example-mintyr_examples}
# List all example files
mintyr_examples()
```

# import_xlsx
    
```{r function-import_xlsx}
#' Import Data from XLSX Files
#'
#' @description
#' A high-performance function for importing data from one or multiple Excel
#' files into \code{data.table} format, with fine-grained control over source
#' tracking columns, sheet selection, and row skipping.
#'
#' Performance characteristics (v3 baseline, unchanged here):
#' \itemize{
#'   \item \code{excel_sheets()} called exactly once per file (cached).
#'   \item \code{setDT()} converts tibbles in-place — zero vector copies.
#'   \item Tracking columns injected via \code{:=} on small per-sheet tables
#'         \emph{before} the single final \code{rbindlist}.
#'   \item \code{data.table} OpenMP thread pool optionally widened and always
#'         restored on exit.
#' }
#'
#' @param file       Non-empty \code{character} vector of paths to existing
#'                   \code{.xlsx} / \code{.xls} files.
#' @param rbind      \code{logical(1)}. \code{TRUE} (default) binds all sheets
#'                   into a single \code{data.table}. \code{FALSE} returns a
#'                   flat named list keyed as \code{"<filename>_<sheetname>"}.
#' @param sheet      Positive \code{integer} vector or \code{NULL} (default).
#'                   \code{NULL} imports every sheet. Indices must be valid
#'                   across all supplied files.
#' @param skip       Non-negative \code{integer(1)}. Number of rows to skip
#'                   before reading the header. Forwarded directly to
#'                   \code{\link[readxl]{read_excel}}. Default \code{0L}.
#' @param show_excel_name \code{logical(1)}. When \code{TRUE} (default) and
#'                   \code{rbind = TRUE}, prepends an \code{excel_name} column
#'                   recording the source filename (extension stripped).
#'                   Silently ignored when \code{rbind = FALSE} (provenance is
#'                   already encoded in list-element names).
#' @param show_sheet_name \code{logical(1)}. When \code{TRUE} (default) and
#'                   \code{rbind = TRUE}, prepends a \code{sheet_name} column
#'                   recording the source sheet. Silently ignored when
#'                   \code{rbind = FALSE}.
#' @param verbose    \code{logical(1)}. Emit a \code{message()} for every
#'                   empty sheet encountered. Default \code{FALSE}.
#' @param dt_threads \code{integer(1)}. OpenMP threads for \code{data.table}
#'                   operations (\code{rbindlist}, \code{:=}, \code{set*}).
#'                   Defaults to the current \code{getDTthreads()} value.
#'                   Always restored on exit.
#' @param ...        Additional arguments forwarded to
#'                   \code{\link[readxl]{read_excel}} (e.g. \code{col_types},
#'                   \code{na}, \code{trim_ws}). \strong{Do not} pass
#'                   \code{sheet} or \code{skip} here; use the dedicated
#'                   parameters above.
#'
#' @return
#' \describe{
#'   \item{\code{rbind = TRUE}}{A \code{data.table}. Tracking columns
#'     \code{excel_name} and/or \code{sheet_name} are prepended when their
#'     respective \code{show_*} flags are \code{TRUE}.}
#'   \item{\code{rbind = FALSE}}{A named \code{list} of \code{data.table}s,
#'     each element named \code{"<filename>_<sheetname>"}. The list carries a
#'     \code{"source_files"} attribute with the original file paths.}
#' }
#'
#' @importFrom data.table setDT setcolorder setattr getDTthreads setDTthreads
#'   rbindlist `:=`
#' @importFrom readxl read_excel excel_sheets
#' @export
import_xlsx <- function(file,
                        rbind           = TRUE,
                        sheet           = NULL,
                        skip            = 0L,
                        show_excel_name = TRUE,
                        show_sheet_name = TRUE,
                        verbose         = FALSE,
                        dt_threads      = data.table::getDTthreads(),
                        ...) {
  excel_name <- sheet_name <- NULL 

  # ── 1. Input validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  # file: non-empty character vector; all paths must exist
  if (!is.character(file) || length(file) == 0L)
    stop("`file` must be a non-empty character vector.", call. = FALSE)

  bad <- file[!file.exists(file)]
  if (length(bad))
    stop("File(s) not found:\n  ", paste(bad, collapse = "\n  "), call. = FALSE)

  # rbind: single non-NA logical
  if (!is.logical(rbind) || length(rbind) != 1L || is.na(rbind))
    stop("`rbind` must be a single non-NA logical value.", call. = FALSE)

  # sheet: NULL or non-empty positive integer vector without NAs
  if (!is.null(sheet)) {
    if (!is.numeric(sheet) || length(sheet) == 0L ||
        anyNA(sheet)        || any(sheet < 1L))
      stop("`sheet` must be a positive integer vector or NULL.", call. = FALSE)
    sheet <- as.integer(sheet)
  }

  # skip: single non-negative integer
  if (!is.numeric(skip) || length(skip) != 1L || is.na(skip) || skip < 0L)
    stop("`skip` must be a single non-negative integer.", call. = FALSE)
  skip <- as.integer(skip)

  # show_excel_name / show_sheet_name: single non-NA logical each
  for (.p in c("show_excel_name", "show_sheet_name")) {
    v <- get(.p)
    if (!is.logical(v) || length(v) != 1L || is.na(v))
      stop(sprintf("`%s` must be a single non-NA logical.", .p), call. = FALSE)
  }

  # dt_threads: single positive integer
  if (!is.numeric(dt_threads) || length(dt_threads) != 1L ||
      is.na(dt_threads)        || dt_threads < 1L)
    stop("`dt_threads` must be a single positive integer.", call. = FALSE)
  dt_threads <- as.integer(dt_threads)

  # Guard: sheet / skip must not be passed through ...
  reserved <- intersect(names(list(...)), c("sheet", "skip"))
  if (length(reserved))
    stop(sprintf(
      "Do not pass %s via `...`; use the dedicated parameter(s) instead.",
      paste0("`", reserved, "`", collapse = " and ")
    ), call. = FALSE)

  # ── 2. Widen data.table thread pool; always restore on exit ───────────────

  old_threads <- data.table::getDTthreads()
  if (dt_threads != old_threads) {
    data.table::setDTthreads(dt_threads)
    on.exit(data.table::setDTthreads(old_threads), add = TRUE)
  }

  # ── 3. Helper: strip file extension from basename ─────────────────────────

  strip_ext <- function(paths) sub("\\.[^.]+$", "", basename(paths))

  # ── 4. Cache sheet directories — one file open per file ───────────────────

  sheets_cache        <- lapply(file, readxl::excel_sheets)
  names(sheets_cache) <- file

  # Validate sheet indices against the smallest sheet count across all files
  if (!is.null(sheet)) {
    min_n <- min(vapply(sheets_cache, length, integer(1L)))
    if (max(sheet) > min_n)
      stop(sprintf(
        "Sheet index %d exceeds the minimum sheet count (%d) across files.",
        max(sheet), min_n), call. = FALSE)
  }

  # ── 5. Build flat (file, sheet) task list ─────────────────────────────────
  #
  # Flat iteration over every (file × sheet) pair; avoids nested lapply and
  # keeps the hot loop in step 6 simple and branch-free.

  file_base <- strip_ext(file)

  tasks <- unlist(
    lapply(seq_along(file), function(i) {
      avail <- sheets_cache[[ file[[i]] ]]
      sel   <- if (is.null(sheet)) avail else avail[sheet]
      lapply(sel, function(sht)
        list(path = file[[i]], base = file_base[[i]], sheet = sht)
      )
    }),
    recursive = FALSE
  )

  # ── 6. Read every (file, sheet) pair ──────────────────────────────────────
  #
  # Micro-optimisations carried over from v3:
  #   • setDT()    — in-place tibble → data.table, zero vector copies.
  #   • :=         — tracking columns injected by reference on small tables.
  #   • do.call    — only invoked when extra `...` args are present.
  #
  # `need_tracking`: TRUE when at least one tracking column will be written.
  # When both show_* flags are FALSE and rbind = TRUE, we skip := entirely.

  dots         <- list(...)
  has_dots     <- length(dots) > 0L
  need_excel   <- rbind && show_excel_name   # only meaningful in combine mode
  need_sheet   <- rbind && show_sheet_name

  sheet_tables <- lapply(tasks, function(tk) {

    # -- Read (skip forwarded explicitly; remaining dots via do.call) ----------
    raw <- if (has_dots) {
      do.call(readxl::read_excel,
              c(list(path = tk$path, sheet = tk$sheet, skip = skip), dots))
    } else {
      readxl::read_excel(tk$path, sheet = tk$sheet, skip = skip)
    }

    # -- In-place tibble → data.table (no vector copy) ------------------------
    dt <- data.table::setDT(raw)

    if (verbose && nrow(dt) == 0L)
      message(sprintf("Note: '%s' / sheet '%s' is empty.",
                      basename(tk$path), tk$sheet))

    # -- Inject only the requested tracking columns by reference --------------
    if (need_excel && need_sheet) {
      dt[, `:=`(excel_name = tk$base, sheet_name = tk$sheet)]
    } else if (need_excel) {
      dt[, excel_name := tk$base]
    } else if (need_sheet) {
      dt[, sheet_name := tk$sheet]
    }

    dt
  })

  # ── 7. Assemble output ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  if (rbind) {

    out <- data.table::rbindlist(sheet_tables, use.names = TRUE, fill = TRUE)

    # Move whichever tracking columns were added to the front
    front_cols <- character(0L)
    if (need_excel) front_cols <- c(front_cols, "excel_name")
    if (need_sheet) front_cols <- c(front_cols, "sheet_name")
    if (length(front_cols))
      data.table::setcolorder(out, front_cols)

    return(out[])

  } else {

    # Named list — provenance lives in element names, not extra columns
    nms <- vapply(tasks,
                  function(tk) paste(tk$base, tk$sheet, sep = "_"),
                  character(1L))

    out <- stats::setNames(sheet_tables, nms)
    data.table::setattr(out, "source_files", file)
    return(out)
  }
}
```
  
```{r example-import_xlsx}
# Example: Excel file import demonstrations

# Setup test files
xlsx_files <- mintyr_example(
  mintyr_examples("xlsx_test")    # Get example Excel files
)

# Example 1: Import and combine all sheets from all files
import_xlsx(
  xlsx_files,                     # Input Excel file paths
  rbind = TRUE                    # Combine all sheets into one data.table
)

# Example 2: Import specific sheets separately
import_xlsx(
  xlsx_files,                     # Input Excel file paths
  rbind = FALSE,                  # Keep sheets as separate data.tables
  sheet = 2                       # Only import first sheet
)
```

# import_csv

```{r function-import_csv}
#' Flexible CSV/TXT File Import via data.table
#'
#' @description
#' Reads one or more CSV/TXT files using \code{\link[data.table]{fread}} as the backend.
#' Supports flexible combination strategies and source-file tracking. All return values
#' are \code{data.table} objects.
#'
#' @param file A non-empty \code{character} vector of file paths to CSV/TXT files.
#'   All paths must point to existing, accessible files.
#' @param rbind A \code{logical} scalar controlling the combination strategy:
#'   \itemize{
#'     \item \code{TRUE} (default): Combine all files into a single \code{data.table}.
#'     \item \code{FALSE}: Return a named \code{list} of individual \code{data.table} objects.
#'   }
#' @param rbind_label A \code{character} scalar or \code{NULL} specifying the source-tracking
#'   column name (default: \code{"_file"}). Set to \code{NULL} to suppress the source column.
#'   Only applies when \code{rbind = TRUE}.
#' @param full_path A \code{logical} scalar controlling path representation in labels:
#'   \itemize{
#'     \item \code{FALSE} (default): Use only the filename (via \code{basename()}).
#'     \item \code{TRUE}: Use the full file path.
#'   }
#' @param keep_ext A \code{logical} scalar controlling whether the file extension is
#'   retained in labels:
#'   \itemize{
#'     \item \code{FALSE} (default): Strip the file extension (e.g., \code{"data"}).
#'     \item \code{TRUE}: Retain the file extension (e.g., \code{"data.csv"}).
#'   }
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed directly to \code{\link[data.table]{fread}}
#'   (e.g., \code{select}, \code{drop}, \code{na.strings}, \code{skip}, \code{nThread}).
#'
#' @details
#' Label generation is controlled by the combination of \code{full_path} and \code{keep_ext}:
#' \tabular{ll}{
#'   \code{full_path = FALSE, keep_ext = FALSE} \tab Filename without extension: \code{"data"} \cr
#'   \code{full_path = FALSE, keep_ext = TRUE}  \tab Filename with extension: \code{"data.csv"} \cr
#'   \code{full_path = TRUE,  keep_ext = FALSE} \tab Full path without extension: \code{"/path/to/data"} \cr
#'   \code{full_path = TRUE,  keep_ext = TRUE}  \tab Full path with extension: \code{"/path/to/data.csv"} \cr
#' }
#'
#' When \code{rbind = TRUE} and \code{rbind_label} is not \code{NULL},
#' \code{\link[data.table]{rbindlist}} is called with \code{idcol = rbind_label},
#' which generates the source column directly during the merge step without any
#' intermediate copies.
#'
#' @return
#' \itemize{
#'   \item \code{rbind = TRUE}: A single \code{data.table} containing all imported rows.
#'     If \code{rbind_label} is not \code{NULL}, the first column contains the source
#'     file label for each row.
#'   \item \code{rbind = FALSE}: A named \code{list} of \code{data.table} objects.
#'     List names are derived from file paths according to \code{full_path} and
#'     \code{keep_ext} settings.
#' }
#'
#' @note
#' \itemize{
#'   \item All specified files must exist and be readable at call time.
#'   \item \code{rbind = TRUE} assumes compatible column structures across files;
#'     mismatched columns are automatically aligned via \code{fill = TRUE}.
#'   \item Logical parameters (\code{rbind}, \code{full_path}, \code{keep_ext}) reject
#'     \code{NA} values explicitly.
#' }
#'
#' @seealso
#' \code{\link[data.table]{fread}}, \code{\link[data.table]{rbindlist}}
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @export
import_csv <- function(
    file,
    rbind       = TRUE,
    rbind_label = "_file",
    full_path   = FALSE,
    keep_ext    = FALSE,
    ...
) {

  # ── 1. Input validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  # `file`: must be a non-empty character vector; report every missing path
  if (!is.character(file) || length(file) == 0L) {
    stop("`file` must be a non-empty character vector.", call. = FALSE)
  }
  missing_files <- file[!file.exists(file)]
  if (length(missing_files) > 0L) {
    stop(
      "The following files do not exist or cannot be accessed:\n",
      paste0("  - ", missing_files, collapse = "\n"),
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }

  # Logical scalars: isTRUE/isFALSE explicitly rejects NA and non-logical types
  if (!isTRUE(rbind) && !isFALSE(rbind)) {
    stop("`rbind` must be TRUE or FALSE (NA is not accepted).", call. = FALSE)
  }
  if (!isTRUE(full_path) && !isFALSE(full_path)) {
    stop("`full_path` must be TRUE or FALSE (NA is not accepted).", call. = FALSE)
  }
  if (!isTRUE(keep_ext) && !isFALSE(keep_ext)) {
    stop("`keep_ext` must be TRUE or FALSE (NA is not accepted).", call. = FALSE)
  }

  # `rbind_label`: NULL disables labeling; otherwise must be a single non-empty string
  if (!is.null(rbind_label) &&
      (!is.character(rbind_label) || length(rbind_label) != 1L ||
       is.na(rbind_label)         || nchar(rbind_label) == 0L)) {
    stop("`rbind_label` must be a single non-empty character string, or NULL.",
         call. = FALSE)
  }

  # ── 2. Label generation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  # Vectorised over all paths at once; called once, not once per file
  make_label <- function(paths) {
    # Step 1 – full path vs basename
    out <- if (full_path) paths else basename(paths)

    # Step 2 – strip extension when keep_ext = FALSE
    # regexpr + substr avoids repeated regex compilation vs sub() in a loop
    if (!keep_ext) {
      dot_pos <- regexpr("\\.[^.]*$", out)   # position of final '.'
      has_ext  <- dot_pos > 0L               # logical mask: only strip when present
      out[has_ext] <- substr(out[has_ext], 1L, dot_pos[has_ext] - 1L)
    }
    out
  }

  # Pre-compute all labels as a named character vector (reused for list names / idcol)
  labels <- make_label(file)

  # ── 3. Read all files ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  # lapply preserves order; names are set immediately for use by rbindlist(idcol)
  file_list        <- lapply(file, data.table::fread, ...)
  names(file_list) <- labels

  # ── 4. Combine or return as list ──────────────────────────────────────────

  if (!rbind) {
    # Return the named list directly; each element is already a data.table
    return(file_list)
  }

  # rbindlist with idcol generates the source column in one pass —
  # no intermediate cbind or data.frame copies
  data.table::rbindlist(
    file_list,
    use.names = TRUE,
    fill      = TRUE,
    idcol     = rbind_label   # NULL silently suppresses the column (rbindlist behaviour)
  )
}
```

```{r examples-import_csv}
# Example: CSV file import demonstrations

# Setup test files
csv_files <- mintyr_example(
  mintyr_examples("csv_test")     # Get example CSV files
)

# Example 1: Import and combine CSV files using data.table
import_csv(
  csv_files,                      # Input CSV file paths
  rbind = TRUE,                   # Combine all files into one data.table
  rbind_label = "_file",          # Column name for file source
  keep_ext = TRUE,                # Include .csv extension in _file column
  full_path = TRUE                # Show complete file paths in _file column
)
```

# w2l_nest
    
```{r function-w2l_nest}
#' Reshape Wide Data to Long Format and Nest by Specified Columns
#'
#' @description
#' `w2l_nest` reshapes a wide-format `data.frame` or `data.table` into long
#' format, then nests the result by `name` (the pivoted column identifier) and
#' any optional grouping variables supplied via `by`. Each row of the returned
#' table contains a nested `data.table` or `data.frame` in the `data` list-column.
#'
#' @param data `data.frame` or `data.table`. Wide-format input dataset.
#'   Converted in-place to `data.table` via `setDT()` if necessary (no copy).
#' @param cols2l `numeric` or `character`. Columns to pivot from wide to long,
#'   specified as integer indices or column names. Default `NULL`: when `NULL`,
#'   `by` must be provided and the function performs a pure nest operation
#'   (no melting).
#' @param by `numeric` or `character`. Additional grouping variables for
#'   hierarchical nesting, specified as integer indices or column names.
#'   Default `NULL`.
#' @param nest_type `character`. Class of each nested subset:
#'   `"dt"` for `data.table` (default) or `"df"` for `data.frame`.
#'
#' @return A `data.table` with one row per combination of `name` (and `by`
#'   levels, if provided). The `data` list-column holds the corresponding
#'   nested `data.table` or `data.frame` for each group. Grouping key columns
#'   are never duplicated inside the nested objects.
#'
#' @details
#' **Column resolution:** both `cols2l` and `by` accept either integer column
#' positions or character column names. Out-of-bounds indices and unknown names
#' are caught early with informative error messages.
#'
#' **Overlap guard:** if any column appears in both `cols2l` and `by`, the
#' function stops with an error before attempting to melt, preventing silent
#' structural corruption.
#'
#' **Factor-free melting:** `melt()` is called with `variable.factor = FALSE`
#' so the `name` column is always `character`, avoiding unexpected factor-level
#' ordering in downstream grouping operations.
#'
#' **Memory efficiency:**
#' \itemize{
#'   \item `setDT()` converts `data.frame` inputs by reference — no full copy.
#'   \item `.SDcols` restricts `.SD` to non-key columns, eliminating redundant
#'         storage of grouping keys inside each nested object.
#'   \item For `nest_type = "df"`, `setattr(copy(.SD), "class", "data.frame")`
#'         modifies the class attribute on a shallow copy rather than performing
#'         a deep column-by-column duplication as `as.data.frame()` would.
#' }
#'
#' @note
#' \itemize{
#'   \item Passing an empty table (0 rows) triggers a `warning()` and returns
#'         an empty nested `data.table` immediately.
#'   \item `cols2l` and `by` must not overlap; overlapping columns will raise
#'         an error.
#'   \item `nest_type` values other than `"dt"` or `"df"` raise an error.
#' }
#'
#' @seealso [`tidytable::nest_by()`] for a tidyverse-style equivalent.
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @export
w2l_nest <- function(data, cols2l = NULL, by = NULL, nest_type = "dt") {

  # ── 0. Internal helper: resolve column indices or names to a character vector
  .resolve_cols <- function(x, data_names, arg_name) {
    if (is.numeric(x)) {
      # Bounds check: all indices must fall within [1, ncol]
      if (any(x < 1L | x > length(data_names))) {
        stop(sprintf(
          "`%s` contains out-of-bounds numeric index/indices (valid range: 1 to %d).",
          arg_name, length(data_names)
        ))
      }
      return(data_names[x])
    }
    if (is.character(x)) {
      # Single-pass missing-column detection using %chin% (character-optimised %in%)
      missing_cols <- x[!x %chin% data_names]
      if (length(missing_cols) > 0L) {
        stop(sprintf(
          "`%s` contains column(s) not found in `data`: %s",
          arg_name, paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", ")
        ))
      }
      return(x)
    }
    stop(sprintf(
      "`%s` must be a numeric index vector or a character column-name vector; got: %s",
      arg_name, class(x)[1L]
    ))
  }
```
