```{r function-w2l_nest}
  # ── 1. Input type validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.data.frame(data) && !data.table::is.data.table(data)) {
    stop("`data` must be a data.frame or a data.table.")
  }

  # Convert by reference: no-op if already a data.table, avoids a full copy otherwise
  data.table::setDT(data)

  # ── 2. Validate nest_type ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!nest_type %chin% c("dt", "df")) {
    stop("`nest_type` must be either 'dt' or 'df'; got: ", nest_type)
  }

  # ── 3. Early return on empty input (prevents silent zero-row output) ────────
  if (nrow(data) == 0L) {
    warning("`data` has 0 rows; returning an empty nested data.table.")
    return(data.table::data.table(name = character(0L), data = list()))
  }

  data_names <- names(data)

  # ── 4. Resolve `by` columns ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.null(by)) {
    by <- .resolve_cols(by, data_names, "by")
  }

  # ── 5. Branch: cols2l is NULL → pure nest mode (no melting) ────────────────
  if (is.null(cols2l)) {
    if (is.null(by)) {
      stop("When `cols2l` is NULL, `by` must be provided.")
    }

    # Columns to nest = all columns that are not grouping keys
    nest_sd_cols <- data_names[!data_names %chin% by]

    result <- .nest_by_group(
      dt        = data,
      group_by  = by,
      sd_cols   = nest_sd_cols,
      nest_type = nest_type
    )
    return(result)
  }

  # ── 6. Resolve `cols2l` and guard against overlap with `by` ────────────────
  cols2l <- .resolve_cols(cols2l, data_names, "cols2l")

  if (!is.null(by)) {
    overlap <- cols2l[cols2l %chin% by]
    if (length(overlap) > 0L) {
      stop(
        "`cols2l` and `by` share column(s), which would corrupt the melt structure: ",
        paste(overlap, collapse = ", ")
      )
    }
  }

  # ── 7. Wide-to-long reshape (melt) ─────────────────────────────────────────
  # variable.factor = FALSE: keep `name` as character to avoid factor-ordering side-effects
  melted <- data.table::melt(
    data,
    measure.vars    = cols2l,
    variable.name   = "name",
    value.name      = "value",
    variable.factor = FALSE   # <- critical: prevents implicit factor coercion
  )

  # ── 8. Determine nesting group keys ────────────────────────────────────────
  # Restrict .SD to non-key columns so grouping keys are never stored redundantly
  # inside each nested object
  group_keys   <- if (!is.null(by)) c("name", by) else "name"
  nest_sd_cols <- names(melted)[!names(melted) %chin% group_keys]

  # ── 9. Nest ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  result <- .nest_by_group(
    dt        = melted,
    group_by  = group_keys,
    sd_cols   = nest_sd_cols,
    nest_type = nest_type
  )

  return(result)
}
```
  
```{r example-w2l_nest}
# Example: Wide to long format nesting demonstrations

# Example 1: Basic nesting by group
w2l_nest(
  data = iris,                    # Input dataset
  by = "Species"                  # Group by Species column
)

# Example 2: Nest specific columns with numeric indices
w2l_nest(
  data = iris,                    # Input dataset
  cols2l = 1:4,                   # Select first 4 columns to nest
  by = "Species"                  # Group by Species column
)

# Example 3: Nest specific columns with column names
w2l_nest(
  data = iris,                    # Input dataset
  cols2l = c("Sepal.Length",      # Select columns by name
             "Sepal.Width", 
             "Petal.Length"),
  by = 5                          # Group by column index 5 (Species)
)
# Returns similar structure to Example 2
```

```{r function-.nest_by_group}
#
# Extracted as a standalone function for two reasons:
#   1. Eliminates duplicated nest_type branching in the main function (DRY).
#   2. Allows isolated unit testing of the nesting logic.
#
# @param dt        A pre-processed data.table ready for nesting.
# @param group_by  Character vector of grouping key column names.
# @param sd_cols   Character vector of columns to include in each nested object.
# @param nest_type "dt" to nest as data.table, "df" to nest as data.frame.
# @return          A data.table with a `data` list-column containing nested subsets.
.nest_by_group <- function(dt, group_by, sd_cols, nest_type) {
  . <- NULL

  if (nest_type == "dt") {
    # .SD is already a data.table; wrapping in list() incurs zero extra allocation
    result <- dt[,
      .(data = list(.SD)),
      by      = group_by,
      .SDcols = sd_cols      # <- pin columns precisely; excludes grouping keys
    ]
  } else {
    # nest_type == "df"
    # setattr() modifies the class on a shallow copy of .SD, avoiding the
    # deep column-vector duplication that as.data.frame() would trigger
    result <- dt[,
      .(data = list(setattr(copy(.SD), "class", "data.frame"))),
      by      = group_by,
      .SDcols = sd_cols
    ]
  }

  return(result)
}
```



# w2l_split
    
```{r function-w2l_split}
#' Reshape Wide Data to Long Format and Split into a Named List
#'
#' @description
#' `w2l_split` reshapes a wide-format `data.frame` or `data.table` into long
#' format, then splits the result into a named list keyed by the pivoted column
#' identifier (`variable`) and any optional grouping variables supplied via
#' `by`. List element names are derived directly from the grouping key
#' combinations produced by `split()`, guaranteeing name-to-content alignment.
#'
#' @param data `data.frame` or `data.table`. Wide-format input dataset.
#'   Converted in-place to `data.table` via `setDT()` if necessary (no copy).
#' @param cols2l `numeric` or `character`. Columns to pivot from wide to long,
#'   specified as integer indices or column names. Default `NULL`: when `NULL`,
#'   `by` must be provided and the function splits the data as-is without
#'   melting.
#' @param by `numeric` or `character`. Additional grouping variables used as
#'   secondary split keys, specified as integer indices or column names.
#'   Default `NULL`.
#' @param split_type `character`. Class of each list element: `"dt"` for
#'   `data.table` (default) or `"df"` for `data.frame`.
#' @param sep `character`. Separator used when concatenating multiple grouping
#'   key values into a single list-element name. Default `"_"`.
#'
#' @return A named list of `data.table` or `data.frame` objects (controlled by
#'   `split_type`). Names reflect the key combination of `variable` (and `by`
#'   levels if provided), joined by `sep`.
#' \itemize{
#'   \item If `by` is `NULL`, the list is keyed by the pivoted column names only.
#'   \item If `by` is specified, the list is keyed by `variable` and all `by`
#'         level combinations.
#' }
#'
#' @details
#' **Name safety:** list names are produced by `data.table::split()` itself
#' using its `by` argument, not reconstructed from raw row order. This
#' eliminates the name-to-content misalignment that arises when `unique()` on
#' the original data and `split()`'s internal sort order diverge.
#'
#' **Column resolution:** both `cols2l` and `by` accept integer column
#' positions or character column names. Out-of-bounds indices and unknown names
#' are caught early with informative error messages.
#'
#' **Overlap guard:** columns appearing in both `cols2l` and `by` raise an
#' error before melting to prevent `id.vars` / `measure.vars` conflicts.
#'
#' **Factor-free melting:** `melt()` is called with `variable.factor = FALSE`
#' so the `variable` column is always `character`, keeping `split()` sort order
#' consistent with lexicographic expectations.
#'
#' **Memory efficiency:**
#' \itemize{
#'   \item `setDT()` converts `data.frame` inputs by reference — no full copy.
#'   \item For `split_type = "df"`, `setattr(copy(x), "class", "data.frame")`
#'         modifies the class on a shallow copy, avoiding the deep
#'         column-by-column duplication that `as.data.frame()` triggers.
#' }
#'
#' @note
#' \itemize{
#'   \item An empty input table (0 rows) triggers a `warning()` and returns an
#'         empty list immediately.
#'   \item `cols2l` and `by` must not overlap; shared columns raise an error.
#'   \item `split_type` values other than `"dt"` or `"df"` raise an error.
#' }
#'
#' @seealso [`tidytable::group_split()`] for a tidyverse-style equivalent.
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @export
w2l_split <- function(data, cols2l = NULL, by = NULL, split_type = "dt", sep = "_") {

  # ── 0. Internal helper: resolve column indices or names to a character vector
  .resolve_cols <- function(x, data_names, arg_name) {
    if (is.numeric(x)) {
      # Bounds check: all indices must fall within [1, ncol]
      if (any(x < 1L | x > length(data_names))) {
        stop(sprintf(
          "`%s` contains out-of-bounds numeric index/indices (valid range: 1 to %d).",
          arg_name, length(data_names)
        ))
      }
      return(data_names[x])
    }
    if (is.character(x)) {
      # Single-pass missing-column detection via %chin% (character-optimised %in%)
      missing_cols <- x[!x %chin% data_names]
      if (length(missing_cols) > 0L) {
        stop(sprintf(
          "`%s` contains column(s) not found in `data`: %s",
          arg_name, paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", ")
        ))
      }
      return(x)
    }
    stop(sprintf(
      "`%s` must be a numeric index vector or a character column-name vector; got: %s",
      arg_name, class(x)[1L]
    ))
  }

  # ── 1. Input type validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.data.frame(data) && !data.table::is.data.table(data)) {
    stop("`data` must be a data.frame or a data.table.")
  }

  # Convert by reference: no-op if already a data.table, avoids a full copy otherwise
  data.table::setDT(data)

  # ── 2. Validate split_type ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!split_type %chin% c("dt", "df")) {
    stop("`split_type` must be either 'dt' or 'df'; got: ", split_type)
  }

  # ── 3. Early return on empty input ─────────────────────────────────────────
  if (nrow(data) == 0L) {
    warning("`data` has 0 rows; returning an empty list.")
    return(list())
  }

  data_names <- names(data)

  # ── 4. Resolve `by` columns ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.null(by)) {
    by <- .resolve_cols(by, data_names, "by")
  }

  # ── 5. Branch: cols2l is NULL → split as-is, no melting ───────────────────
  if (is.null(cols2l)) {
    if (is.null(by)) {
      stop("When `cols2l` is NULL, `by` must be provided.")
    }

    # split() names each element after the key combination automatically;
    # keep.by = FALSE drops the key columns from each list element
    dt_list <- split(data, by = by, keep.by = FALSE, drop = TRUE, sep = sep)
    return(.convert_split_type(dt_list, split_type))
  }

  # ── 6. Resolve `cols2l` and guard against overlap with `by` ────────────────
  cols2l <- .resolve_cols(cols2l, data_names, "cols2l")

  if (!is.null(by)) {
    overlap <- cols2l[cols2l %chin% by]
    if (length(overlap) > 0L) {
      stop(
        "`cols2l` and `by` share column(s), which would corrupt the melt structure: ",
        paste(overlap, collapse = ", ")
      )
    }
  }

  # ── 7. Wide-to-long reshape (melt) ─────────────────────────────────────────
  # variable.factor = FALSE: keep `variable` as character, ensuring split()
  # sort order matches lexicographic expectations
  dt_long <- data.table::melt(
    data,
    measure.vars    = cols2l,
    variable.name   = "variable",
    value.name      = "value",
    variable.factor = FALSE   # <- critical: prevents implicit factor coercion
  )

  # ── 8. Determine split keys and split ──────────────────────────────────────
  # split() constructs names from key combinations internally — no manual
  # paste() reconstruction needed, which was the source of the original
  # name-misalignment bug
  split_keys <- if (!is.null(by)) c("variable", by) else "variable"

  dt_list <- split(dt_long, by = split_keys, keep.by = FALSE, drop = TRUE, sep = sep)

  # ── 9. Convert to requested output type and return ─────────────────────────
  return(.convert_split_type(dt_list, split_type))
}
```
  
```{r example-w2l_split}
# Example: Wide to long format splitting demonstrations

# Example 1: Basic splitting by Species
w2l_split(
  data = iris,                    # Input dataset
  by = "Species"                  # Split by Species column
) |> 
  lapply(head)                    # Show first 6 rows of each split

# Example 2: Split specific columns using numeric indices
w2l_split(
  data = iris,                    # Input dataset
  cols2l = 1:3,                   # Select first 3 columns to split
  by = 5                          # Split by column index 5 (Species)
) |> 
  lapply(head)                    # Show first 6 rows of each split

# Example 3: Split specific columns using column names
list_res <- w2l_split(
  data = iris,                    # Input dataset
  cols2l = c("Sepal.Length",      # Select columns by name
             "Sepal.Width"),
  by = "Species"                  # Split by Species column
)
lapply(list_res, head)            # Show first 6 rows of each split
# Returns similar structure to Example 2
```


```{r function-.convert_split_type}
#
# Using setattr(copy(x), "class", "data.frame") instead of as.data.frame(x)
# avoids a deep column-vector duplication on every list element, significantly
# reducing GC pressure when the list has many elements.
#
# @param dt_list   Named list of data.table objects produced by split().
# @param split_type "dt" to keep as data.table, "df" to convert to data.frame.
# @return          The same named list with elements of the requested class.
.convert_split_type <- function(dt_list, split_type) {
  if (split_type == "df") {
    # Shallow-copy + class reassignment: avoids deep duplication of column vectors
    dt_list <- lapply(dt_list, function(x) setattr(copy(x), "class", "data.frame"))
  }
  # split_type == "dt": elements are already data.tables; nothing to do
  dt_list
}
```

# nest_cv
    
```{r function-nest_cv}
#' Apply Cross-Validation to Nested Data
#'
#' @description
#' `nest_cv` applies `rsample::vfold_cv` to each nested data frame within a
#' `data.table`, returning an expanded result table containing the corresponding
#' training and validation splits for each row.
#'
#' @param nest_dt A `data.frame` or `data.table` containing at least one nested
#'   `data.frame`/`data.table` column.
#' @param v       Number of folds. Must be an integer >= 2. Default is `10`.
#' @param repeats Number of repeats. Must be an integer >= 1. Default is `1`.
#' @param strata  A single character string specifying the stratification column
#'   name. Set to `NULL` for no stratification. Default is `NULL`.
#' @param breaks  Number of bins for stratifying a numeric variable. Only used
#'   when `strata` is non-`NULL`. Default is `4`.
#' @param pool    Proportion threshold for pooling small strata. Only used when
#'   `strata` is non-`NULL`. Default is `0.1`.
#' @param ...     Additional arguments passed to `rsample::vfold_cv`.
#'
#' @details
#' The function performs the following steps:
#' \enumerate{
#'   \item Validates that `nest_dt` is a non-empty `data.frame` or `data.table`
#'         with at least one nested column whose elements all inherit from
#'         `data.frame`.
#'   \item Selects the target nested column: prefers a column named `"data"`;
#'         otherwise falls back to the first detected nested column.
#'   \item When `strata` is specified, verifies that the column exists in every
#'         nested data frame before calling `rsample::vfold_cv`.
#'   \item Iterates over each row, applies `vfold_cv` via `do.call`, expands the
#'         resulting splits into a `data.table`, and broadcasts the row's
#'         non-nested metadata columns across all CV rows.
#'   \item Combines all per-row results with `rbindlist` in a single pass.
#' }
#'
#' @return A `data.table` with the following columns:
#' \itemize{
#'   \item All non-nested columns from `nest_dt` (broadcast across CV rows).
#'   \item `splits`   — cross-validation split objects from `rsample::vfold_cv`.
#'   \item `id` (and `id2` for repeated CV) — fold identifiers.
#'   \item `train`    — list column of training data frames for each split.
#'   \item `validate` — list column of validation data frames for each split.
#' }
#'
#' @note
#' \itemize{
#'   \item `nest_dt` must contain at least one nested column of `data.frame`s
#'         or `data.table`s.
#'   \item `as.data.table()` is used instead of `data.table::copy()`: if the
#'         input is already a `data.table`, no copy is made.
#'   \item `strata` must be a column name present in **all** nested data frames.
#'   \item `breaks` and `pool` are forwarded to `rsample::vfold_cv` only when
#'         `strata` is non-`NULL`, avoiding invalid argument errors.
#'   \item The per-row loop with `rbindlist` corrects a silent bug in naive
#'         chained `[` approaches where all rows incorrectly shared the first
#'         row's CV splits.
#' }
#'
#' @seealso
#' \itemize{
#'   \item [`rsample::vfold_cv()`]  — underlying cross-validation function
#'   \item [`rsample::training()`]  — extract training set from a split
#'   \item [`rsample::testing()`]   — extract test/validation set from a split
#' }
#'
#' @import data.table
#' @importFrom rsample vfold_cv training testing
#' @export
nest_cv <- function(nest_dt,
                    v       = 10L,
                    repeats = 1L,
                    strata  = NULL,
                    breaks  = 4L,
                    pool    = 0.1,
                    ...) {

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  0. Declare local variables to suppress R CMD CHECK global binding notes
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  splits <- train <- validate <- NULL

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  1. Input validation
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  if (!inherits(nest_dt, "data.frame")) {
    stop("`nest_dt` must be a data.frame or data.table. Received: ",
         paste(class(nest_dt), collapse = "/"))
  }

  if (nrow(nest_dt) == 0L) {
    stop("`nest_dt` must not be empty (0 rows).")
  }

  if (!is.numeric(v) || length(v) != 1L || v < 2L) {
    stop("`v` must be a single integer >= 2.")
  }

  if (!is.numeric(repeats) || length(repeats) != 1L || repeats < 1L) {
    stop("`repeats` must be a single integer >= 1.")
  }

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  2. Identify nested columns
  #     A column qualifies if it is a list whose every element inherits from
  #     "data.frame" (data.table inherits from data.frame, so both are covered)
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  nested_cols <- names(nest_dt)[vapply(nest_dt, function(col) {
    is.list(col) &&
      length(col) > 0L &&
      all(vapply(col, inherits, logical(1L), what = "data.frame"))
  }, logical(1L))]

  if (length(nested_cols) == 0L) {
    stop("`nest_dt` contains no nested data.frame/data.table columns.\n",
         "  Column types detected: ",
         paste(names(nest_dt),
               vapply(nest_dt, function(x) class(x)[1L], character(1L)),
               sep = "=", collapse = ", "))
  }

  # Prefer a column named "data"; fall back to the first nested column
  target_col <- if ("data" %in% nested_cols) "data" else nested_cols[1L]

  if (target_col != "data") {
    message("[nest_cv] No nested column named 'data' found. ",
            "Using '", target_col, "' instead.",
            "\n          Available nested columns: ",
            paste(nested_cols, collapse = ", "))
  }

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  3. Validate that the strata column exists in every nested data frame
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  if (!is.null(strata)) {
    if (!is.character(strata) || length(strata) != 1L) {
      stop("`strata` must be a single character string (column name).")
    }

    strata_missing <- vapply(nest_dt[[target_col]], function(df) {
      !strata %in% names(df)
    }, logical(1L))

    if (any(strata_missing)) {
      stop("The `strata` column '", strata, "' is absent from nested ",
           "data frame(s) at row(s): ",
           paste(which(strata_missing), collapse = ", "), ".")
    }
  }

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  4. Coerce to data.table
  #     as.data.table() is a no-op when the input is already a data.table,
  #     avoiding the full deep copy that data.table::copy() would incur
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  dt <- data.table::as.data.table(nest_dt)

  # Non-nested column names used for metadata broadcasting
  non_nested_cols <- setdiff(names(dt), nested_cols)

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  5. Build the vfold_cv argument list
  #     strata-specific arguments (breaks, pool) are appended only when
  #     strata is non-NULL, preventing invalid-argument errors in rsample
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  cv_args <- c(
    list(v = v, repeats = repeats),
    if (!is.null(strata)) list(strata = strata, breaks = breaks, pool = pool),
    list(...)
  )

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  6. Core logic: expand CV splits row-by-row, then combine
  #
  #  The original code used `dt[, cv_split[[1]], by = non_nested_cols]`,
  #  which silently applied the *first* row's splits to every row when
  #  nest_dt had more than one row. The row-wise loop below fixes that.
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  n_rows      <- nrow(dt)
  result_list <- vector("list", n_rows)

  for (i in seq_len(n_rows)) {

    # Extract the nested data frame for row i
    nested_data_i <- dt[[target_col]][[i]]

    # Generate CV splits for this nested data frame
    cv_result_i <- do.call(
      rsample::vfold_cv,
      c(list(data = nested_data_i), cv_args)
    )

    # Convert rsample tibble to data.table (no tibble dependency retained)
    cv_dt_i <- data.table::as.data.table(cv_result_i)

    # Append training and validation list-columns in place (modify by reference)
    cv_dt_i[, train    := lapply(splits, rsample::training)]
    cv_dt_i[, validate := lapply(splits, rsample::testing )]

    # Broadcast the non-nested metadata from row i across all CV rows
    if (length(non_nested_cols) > 0L) {
      n_cv       <- nrow(cv_dt_i)
      meta_i     <- dt[i, ..non_nested_cols]
      # rep() ensures explicit row replication; avoids implicit recycling risks
      meta_expanded <- meta_i[rep(1L, n_cv)]
      result_list[[i]] <- data.table::setcolorder(
        cbind(meta_expanded, cv_dt_i),
        c(non_nested_cols, setdiff(names(cv_dt_i), non_nested_cols))
      )
    } else {
      result_list[[i]] <- cv_dt_i
    }
  }

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  #  7. Combine all per-row results in a single rbindlist pass
  #     use.names = TRUE  : align by column name (guards against column reorder)
  #     fill      = FALSE : all rows must have identical structure
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
  data.table::rbindlist(result_list, use.names = TRUE, fill = FALSE)
}
```
  
```{r example-nest_cv}
# Example: Cross-validation for nested data.table demonstrations

# Setup test data
dt_nest <- w2l_nest(
  data = iris,                   # Input dataset
  cols2l = 1:2                   # Nest first 2 columns
)

# Example 1: Basic 2-fold cross-validation
nest_cv(
  nest_dt = dt_nest,             # Input nested data.table
  v = 2                          # Number of folds (2-fold CV)
)

# Example 2: Repeated 2-fold cross-validation
nest_cv(
  nest_dt = dt_nest,             # Input nested data.table
  v = 2,                         # Number of folds (2-fold CV)
  repeats = 2                    # Number of repetitions
)
```

# top_perc
    
```{r function-top_perc}
#' Select Top or Bottom Percentage of Data
#'
#' @description
#' Selects the top (largest) or bottom (smallest) percentage of data based on
#' specified traits. Positive percentages extract the largest values; negative
#' percentages extract the smallest values.
#'
#' @param data A data.frame or data.table.
#' @param perc A numeric vector strictly between -1 and 1 (excluding 0).
#'   Positive values (e.g., 0.05) select the top X% of largest values.
#'   Negative values (e.g., -0.1) select the bottom X% of smallest values.
#' @param trait A character vector of column names to analyse.
#' @param by A character vector of column names to group by. Default is NULL.
#' @param keep_data Logical. If TRUE, returns a named list where each element
#'   contains both \code{stat} (summary statistics) and \code{data} (the
#'   subset rows). If FALSE (default), returns a single combined data.frame of
#'   statistics for all \code{perc} values.
#'
#' @return
#' \itemize{
#'   \item \code{keep_data = FALSE}: a \code{data.frame} with one row per
#'     \code{by} / \code{trait} / \code{perc} combination, containing columns
#'     \code{n, min, max, mean, median, sd, se, cv, selection}.
#'   \item \code{keep_data = TRUE}: a named list (one element per \code{perc}
#'     value) where each element is a list with \code{$stat} and \code{$data}.
#' }
#'
#' @importFrom data.table as.data.table melt frank setkeyv rbindlist
#' @importFrom stats sd median
#' @export
top_perc <- function(data, perc, trait, by = NULL, keep_data = FALSE) {
  . <- value <- selection <- NULL

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 1. Argument validation
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  missing_args <- character(0)
  if (missing(data))  missing_args <- c(missing_args, "data")
  if (missing(perc))  missing_args <- c(missing_args, "perc")
  if (missing(trait)) missing_args <- c(missing_args, "trait")
  if (length(missing_args) > 0L)
    stop("Missing required argument(s): ", paste(missing_args, collapse = ", "))

  perc <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(perc))
  if (length(perc) == 0L || anyNA(perc))
    stop("'perc' must be a non-empty numeric vector with no NA values.")
  if (any(perc <= -1 | perc >= 1 | perc == 0))
    stop("Each element of 'perc' must be strictly between -1 and 1, and cannot be 0.")

  if (!is.character(trait) || length(trait) == 0L)
    stop("'trait' must be a non-empty character vector.")

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 2. Convert to data.table (no unconditional copy — we only read)
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  dt <- data.table::as.data.table(data)

  missing_traits <- trait[!trait %in% names(dt)]
  if (length(missing_traits) > 0L)
    stop("Column(s) not found in 'data': ", paste(missing_traits, collapse = ", "))

  if (!is.null(by)) {
    if (!is.character(by) || length(by) == 0L)
      stop("'by' must be a non-empty character vector.")
    missing_by <- by[!by %in% names(dt)]
    if (length(missing_by) > 0L)
      stop("Grouping column(s) not found in 'data': ", paste(missing_by, collapse = ", "))
  }

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 3. Reshape to long format ONCE, outside the perc loop
  #    na.rm = TRUE: NAs are excluded uniformly before ranking and
  #    statistics, avoiding ambiguity between melt and frank behaviour.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  id_cols <- setdiff(names(dt), trait)

  dt_long <- data.table::melt(
    dt,
    id.vars       = id_cols,
    measure.vars  = trait,
    variable.name = "variable",
    value.name    = "value",
    na.rm         = TRUE       # unified NA exclusion point
  )

  groupby <- if (is.null(by)) "variable" else c(by, "variable")

  # Pre-build a key so subset operations are fast for all perc iterations
  data.table::setkeyv(dt_long, groupby)

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 4. Iterate over perc values
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  results <- lapply(perc, function(p) {

    abs_p <- abs(p)

    # FIX: ranking is computed *within* each group so that .N refers to
    # the group size, not the total table length.  The previous
    # implementation called .I[ranks <= .N * abs_p] in an outer by=
    # clause where .N was the global row count, producing incorrect
    # cut-offs whenever by != NULL.
    idx <- dt_long[
      ,
      .I[{
        r <- if (p > 0L)
          data.table::frank(-value, ties.method = "min", na.last = "keep")
        else
          data.table::frank( value, ties.method = "min", na.last = "keep")
        # na.last = "keep" → NA ranks are NA → NA <= threshold is NA →
        # those rows are not selected (safe, consistent with na.rm = TRUE
        # applied during melt).
        r <= (.N * abs_p)
      }],
      by = groupby
    ]$V1

    sub_dt <- dt_long[idx]

    # Summary statistics per group
    stats_dt <- sub_dt[
      ,
      {
        n_val <- .N
        if (n_val == 0L) {
          .(N      = 0L,
            Min    = NA_real_,
            Max    = NA_real_,
            Mean   = NA_real_,
            Median = NA_real_,
            SD     = NA_real_,
            SE     = NA_real_,
            CV     = NA_real_)
        } else {
          m <- mean(value)
          s <- sd(value)
          .(N      = n_val,
            Min    = min(value),
            Max    = max(value),
            Mean   = m,
            Median = median(value),
            SD     = s,
            SE     = s / sqrt(n_val),
            CV     = s / m)
        }
      },
      by = groupby
    ]

    # FIX: use sprintf to avoid floating-point display artefacts such as
    # 0.1 * 100 → "10.000000000001".  %.4g trims trailing zeros cleanly.
    label <- if (p > 0)
      sprintf("top_%.4g%%",    abs_p * 100)
    else
      sprintf("bottom_%.4g%%", abs_p * 100)

    stats_dt[, selection := label]

    if (keep_data) {
      list(stat = as.data.frame(stats_dt),
           data = as.data.frame(sub_dt))
    } else {
      as.data.frame(stats_dt)
    }
  })

  names(results) <- paste0("perc_", perc)

  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # 5. Return
  # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  if (keep_data) {
    return(results)
  } else {
    return(as.data.frame(data.table::rbindlist(results)))
  }
}
```
  
```{r example-top_perc}
# Example 1: Basic usage with single trait
# This example selects the top 10% of observations based on Petal.Width
# keep_data=TRUE returns both summary statistics and the filtered data
top_perc(iris, 
         perc = 0.1,                # Select top 10%
         trait = c("Petal.Width"),  # Column to analyze
         keep_data = TRUE)          # Return both stats and filtered data

# Example 2: Using grouping with 'by' parameter
# This example performs the same analysis but separately for each Species
# Returns nested list with stats and filtered data for each group
top_perc(iris, 
         perc = 0.1,                # Select top 10%
         trait = c("Petal.Width"),  # Column to analyze
         by = "Species")            # Group by Species
```

# get_path_info
    
```{r function-get_path_info}
#' Extract Path Segments or Filenames from File Paths
#'
#' @description
#' `get_path_info` is a merged, upgraded replacement for `get_path_segment` and
#' `get_filename`. It operates in two modes:
#'   - **Mode A (when `n` is specified)**: Extract a specific path segment by
#'     position, supporting forward indexing, reverse indexing, and range extraction.
#'   - **Mode B (when `n = NULL`)**: Extract the filename, with optional removal
#'     of the file extension and/or the directory prefix.
#'
#' @param paths A `character` vector of file system paths.
#'   Supports mixed separators (`/` and `\\`) and Windows drive letters (e.g. `C:`).
#'
#' @param n A `numeric` segment index. Defaults to `NULL` (enters filename mode).
#'   - Positive integer: forward index from the path start; `1` = first segment.
#'   - Negative integer: reverse index from the path end; `-1` = last segment
#'     (i.e. the filename segment).
#'   - Length-2 vector: extract a contiguous range, e.g. `c(2, 4)` or `c(-3, -1)`.
#'   - `0` is not allowed.
#'
#' @param rm_extension A `logical(1)` flag controlling extension removal.
#'   Defaults to `TRUE`.
#'   - In Mode B (`n = NULL`): always applied.
#'   - In Mode A: **only applied when `n == -1`** (explicitly targeting the filename
#'     segment). Has no effect for intermediate directory segments (e.g. `n = 2`).
#'
#' @param rm_path A `logical(1)` flag controlling whether the directory prefix is
#'   stripped, keeping only the filename. Defaults to `TRUE`.
#'   Only applies in Mode B (`n = NULL`); ignored when `n` is specified.
#'
#' @return A `character` vector of the same length as `paths`:
#'   - Returns the extracted segment string when the segment exists.
#'   - Returns `NA_character_` when the segment index exceeds the path depth,
#'     the input element is `NA`, or the path reduces to empty after normalisation
#'     (e.g. `"C:/"`, `"/"`).
#'
#' @details
#' **Path normalisation (internal, fully vectorised):**
#' 1. All backslashes and consecutive slashes are collapsed to a single `/`.
#' 2. Windows drive letter prefixes (`C:`, `D:`, etc.) are stripped.
#' 3. Leading and trailing `/` characters are removed.
#' 4. Paths that are empty after the above steps (e.g. original inputs `"C:/"`,
#'    `"/"`, `""`) are coerced to `NA_character_`.
#'
#' **Extension-stripping behaviour (internal `.strip_ext` helper):**
#' | Input             | Output          | Notes                                      |
#' |-------------------|-----------------|--------------------------------------------|
#' | `"report.txt"`    | `"report"`      | Standard file — last extension removed     |
#' | `"data.tar.gz"`   | `"data.tar"`    | Compound extension — only last level removed|
#' | `".bashrc"`       | `".bashrc"`     | Pure dot-file (no second dot) — unchanged  |
#' | `".report.xlsx"`  | `".report"`     | Dot-file with extension — extension removed|
#' | `"no_ext"`        | `"no_ext"`      | No extension — returned as-is              |
#' | `"file."`         | `"file."`       | Trailing isolated dot — returned as-is     |
#'
#' **NA safety:**
#' `strsplit(NA_character_, ...)` returns `list(NA)` with `length` 1, not
#' `character(0)`. Consequently, every `vapply` callback guards against NA paths
#' with an explicit `anyNA(x)` check rather than `length(x) == 0`.
#'
#' @seealso [base::basename()], [tools::file_path_sans_ext()]
#' @export
get_path_info <- function(paths,
                          n            = NULL,
                          rm_extension = TRUE,
                          rm_path      = TRUE) {

  # ── 1. Input validation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (missing(paths))
    stop("Argument 'paths' is missing", call. = FALSE)
  if (!is.character(paths))
    stop("'paths' must be a character vector", call. = FALSE)
  if (length(paths) == 0L)
    return(character(0L))

  if (!is.null(n)) {
    if (!is.numeric(n))
      stop("'n' must be numeric", call. = FALSE)
    if (length(n) > 2L)
      stop("'n' must be a single integer or a length-2 integer vector", call. = FALSE)
    if (any(n == 0))
      stop("'n' must not contain 0", call. = FALSE)
    n <- as.integer(n)  # Coerce to integer to eliminate floating-point comparison risk
  }

  if (!is.logical(rm_extension) || length(rm_extension) != 1L)
    stop("'rm_extension' must be a single logical value", call. = FALSE)
  if (!is.logical(rm_path) || length(rm_path) != 1L)
    stop("'rm_path' must be a single logical value", call. = FALSE)

  # Both flags FALSE with no 'n': no transformation will occur — warn the caller
  if (is.null(n) && !rm_extension && !rm_path)
    warning(
      "Both 'rm_extension' and 'rm_path' are FALSE and 'n' is not specified; ",
      "returning normalised paths unchanged.",
      call. = FALSE
    )

  # ── 2. Path normalisation (vectorised, order-sensitive) ──────────────────────
  # Step order is critical: the drive letter must be stripped BEFORE leading
  # slashes are removed, otherwise "C:/" would lose its "/" first and leave
  # "C:" which no longer matches the drive-letter pattern.
  norm <- gsub("\\\\+|/+", "/", paths)   # ① Backslashes & repeated slashes → single "/"
  norm <- sub("^[A-Za-z]:", "", norm)     # ② Strip Windows drive letter (C:, D:, …)
  norm <- gsub("^/+|/+$", "", norm)       # ③ Remove leading and trailing slashes
  # Paths that are empty after normalisation ("C:/", "/", "", …) become NA
  norm[!is.na(norm) & norm == ""] <- NA_character_

  # ── 3. Path splitting ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Important: strsplit(NA_character_) returns list(NA) with length 1, NOT
  # character(0). Downstream code must therefore use anyNA(x), not length(x) == 0,
  # to detect NA paths safely.
  segs <- strsplit(norm, "/", fixed = TRUE)

  # ── 4. Internal helper: strip the last file extension (vectorised) ───────────
  # Logic (two-layer ifelse — explicit and regex-free for the NA/dot-file cases):
  #   Layer 1: NA or empty string → pass through unchanged.
  #   Layer 2: grepl("^\\.[^.]*$") matches "dot-files with no second dot"
  #            (e.g. ".bashrc", ".gitignore") → leave unchanged.
  #            All other inputs → sub("\\.[^.]+$", "") removes the last ".xxx".
  #
  # Note: [^.]+ in the sub pattern requires at least one non-dot character after
  # the dot, so "file." (trailing isolated dot) is not matched and passes through.
  .strip_ext <- function(x) {
    ifelse(
      is.na(x) | x == "",
      x,
      ifelse(
        grepl("^\\.[^.]*$", x, perl = TRUE),  # Pure dot-file (.bashrc class)
        x,
        sub("\\.[^.]+$", "", x)               # Remove last extension (handles compound too)
      )
    )
  }

  # ── 5. Branching logic ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.null(n)) {
    # ── Mode A: segment extraction ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    if (length(n) == 1L) {
      # Single index: forward (n > 0) or reverse (n < 0)
      result <- vapply(segs, FUN.VALUE = NA_character_, function(x) {
        if (anyNA(x)) return(NA_character_)   # NA path — short-circuit immediately
        len <- length(x)
        pos <- if (n > 0L) n else len + n + 1L
        if (pos >= 1L && pos <= len) x[[pos]] else NA_character_
      })

      # rm_extension linkage: by convention, only applied when n == -1L, which
      # unambiguously targets the filename segment. Stripping an extension from
      # an intermediate directory segment (e.g. n = 2) is semantically meaningless.
      if (n == -1L && rm_extension) {
        result <- .strip_ext(result)
      }

    } else {
      # Range extraction: collapse segments [pos1, pos2] joined by "/"
      result <- vapply(segs, FUN.VALUE = NA_character_, function(x) {
        if (anyNA(x)) return(NA_character_)
        len  <- length(x)
        pos1 <- if (n[1L] > 0L) n[1L] else len + n[1L] + 1L
        pos2 <- if (n[2L] > 0L) n[2L] else len + n[2L] + 1L
        # Auto-sort endpoints so the caller may supply them in any order
        if (pos1 > pos2) { tmp <- pos1; pos1 <- pos2; pos2 <- tmp }
        # Strict boundary check: either endpoint out-of-range → NA
        if (pos1 >= 1L && pos2 <= len) {
          paste(x[pos1:pos2], collapse = "/")
        } else {
          NA_character_
        }
      })
    }

  } else {
    # ── Mode B: filename mode (n = NULL) ─────────────────────────────────────────
    if (rm_path) {
      # Extract only the last path segment (equivalent to basename on normalised paths)
      result <- vapply(segs, FUN.VALUE = NA_character_, function(x) {
        if (anyNA(x)) return(NA_character_)
        x[[length(x)]]
      })
      if (rm_extension) result <- .strip_ext(result)

    } else {
      # Retain the full normalised path, optionally stripping the final segment's extension
      if (rm_extension) {
        result <- vapply(segs, FUN.VALUE = NA_character_, function(x) {
          if (anyNA(x)) return(NA_character_)
          len      <- length(x)
          x[[len]] <- .strip_ext(x[[len]])  # Only the filename segment (last) is modified
          paste(x, collapse = "/")
        })
      } else {
        result <- vapply(segs, FUN.VALUE = NA_character_, function(x) {
          if (anyNA(x)) NA_character_ else paste(x, collapse = "/")
        })
      }
    }
  }

  # vapply already guarantees a character vector; explicit coercion is a final safeguard
  as.character(result)
}
```

```{r example-get_path_info}
paths <- c("C:/Users/foo/Documents/report.xlsx",
           "/home/user/.bashrc",
           "relative/path/to/data.csv",
           ".hidden.tar.gz",
           NA_character_)

# Mode B: filename only, extension stripped (default)
get_path_info(paths)

# Mode B: filename only, extension preserved
get_path_info(paths, rm_extension = FALSE)

# Mode B: full normalised path, extension stripped
get_path_info(paths, rm_path = FALSE)

# Mode A: extract the 2nd path segment
get_path_info(paths, n = 2)

# Mode A: extract the last segment with extension stripped (n = -1 linkage)
get_path_info(paths, n = -1, rm_extension = TRUE)

# Mode A: range extraction
get_path_info(paths, n = c(2, 3))
```


<!-- 
# Inflate your package

You're one inflate from paper to box.
Build your package from this very Rmd using `fusen::inflate()` 
-->


```{r development-inflate, eval=FALSE}
# Execute in the console directly
fusen::inflate(flat_file = "dev/flat_teaching.Rmd", overwrite = T, clean = TRUE)
```

<!-- 
- Verify your `"DESCRIPTION"` file has been updated
- Verify your function is in `"R/"` directory
- Verify your test is in `"tests/testthat/"` directory
- Verify this Rmd appears in `"vignettes/"` directory 
-->
